entry.title: From Blueprint to Reality: SAP BDC Data Architecture Deep Dive Miniseries- June 2026
entry.content:
The SAP Business Data Cloud Data Architecture Deep Dive Miniseries is a focused, four-episode live session program running from June 1-5, 2026, designed exclusively for SAP Partners.

Each session is 1 hour, delivered online, and packed with practical guidance, demos, and strategic insights. Whether you’re just getting started or looking to sharpen your technical edge, this miniseries has something for every experience level.

Register Now

To utilize this opportunity to build your SAP BDC architecture expertise. Sessions are available in multiple timezones.

Date

LSC Registration Link (or)

Alternative link (PE)

01-Jun-26

Data Architect curriculum and SAP Architecture Center

PSD_WEB_00010833

02-Jun-26

Unpacking the Knowledge Core for Data Architects

PSD_WEB_00010852

03-Jun-26

SAP BW and HANA Modernization as a Data Architect

PSD_WEB_00010862

05-Jun-26

Leveraging the Open Data Ecosystem To Drive Success

PSD_WEB_00010869

 

Episode 1- June 1, 2026: Data Architect curriculum and SAP Architecture Center 

Kick off the series with an introduction to the SAP Architecture Center, a powerful resource hub now live and with SAP Business Data Cloud at its core.

In this opening session:

  • What the SAP Architecture Center offers — solution reference architectures, diagrams, scenarios, component lists, and use case examples
  • How SAP Partners can leverage it for top SAP BDC use cases, including Enterprise Data & Analytics Strategy, SAP BW Modernization, SAP Databricks integration, and AI for SAP BDC
  • How to integrate these resources into your Enterprise Architect toolchain (draw.io and more)
  • How SAP Partners can actively contribute to the Architecture Center

Episode 2- June 2, 2026: Data Architect Miniseries - Unpacking the Knowledge Core for Data Architects 

Dive deep into the technical and strategic architecture of SAP Business Data Cloud in this expert session tailored for solution architects.

Key topics covered:

  • Introduction to SAP BDC and its role in unifying SAP and non-SAP data — structured and unstructured — with built-in governance, analytics, and AI
  •  Data products as reusable, governed assets and a strategic approach to data management
  • Technical architecture highlights: zero-copy sharing, object store persistence, and SAP Databricks integration
  • Live demo of an SAP BDC-powered intelligent application
  • Overview of the Data Product Catalog, metadata management, and lifecycle features
  • How SAP Partners can add unique value and best practices

Episode 3- June 3, 2026: Data Architect Miniseries - SAP BW and HANA Modernization as a Data Architect

SAP BW modernization is one of the most critical journeys for many organizations today. This session provides solution architects with clear pathways and practical guidance for modernizing SAP BW and integrating with SAP BDC.

Key topics include:

  • The strategic value of SAP BW modernization - simplified architecture, greater agility, and readiness for AI-driven use cases
  • A comprehensive overview of SAP BW Modernization pathways
  • Common use case patterns where SAP BW queries and models are transformed into agile SAP BDC data products, enabling advanced dashboards and AI/ML capabilities
  • Details on the Free SAP BW to SAP BDC Migration Assessment — a tailored migration road map for your landscape
  • How to add unique value and best practices as an SAP Partner

Episode 4- June 5, 2026: Leveraging the Open Data Ecosystem To Drive Success 

Close out the miniseries with a strategic session focused on architecture options and transition planning for moving to SAP Business Data Cloud — enabling unified data operations, faster innovation cycles, and insight-driven decision-making.

Key topics include:

  •  Transition Scenario Benefits and value realization frameworks
  •  SAP HANA Cloud + SAP BDC — bi-directional data exchange to power hybrid innovation
  •  Greenfield or Migration — flexible paths for both fresh starts and phased modernization
  • Seamless connection of data products with SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP Databricks, and more
  • SAP’s complimentary assessments and services to help you plan and move confidently
  • Strategic roadmap and practical steps for a smooth, successful transition

Who Should Attend?

This miniseries is designed for: Architects | Consultants | Presales | Project Managers | Data Scientists | Data Analysts | Administrators

Direct links are available in this blog or Visit SAP Learning or Partner Portal to find and register for each session.

 

✨Surprise:  

Attend all four episodes of this miniseries and earn an exclusive digital badge 🏆 

#SAPBusinessDataCloud #SAPArchitecture #DataArchitecture #SAPPartner #SAPBDC #SAPBWModernization #DataProducts #SAPDatabricks #SAPAnalytics #CloudData #EnterpriseArchitecture

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud Dimension Tables Move to the File Repository
entry.content:
Introduction

Dimension tables in SAP Analytics Cloud are moving to the file repository. This completes the journey that started with stories, models, data actions, multi actions and currency conversion tables — and it brings with it a significantly simpler approach to privilege management, a cleaner user experience, and a unified way to organize all your planning artifacts in one place.

In this post we walk through what is changing, what the new experience looks like, and what you need to know about the central migration that will move your existing dimensions automatically. This enhancement is shipped with the QRC3.2026 upgrade on QRC and preview systems and with wave 2026.11 on fast-track tenants.

 

Why This Matters: Key Benefits

All in one place

Dimension tables are the final object type to make the move. With this release, stories, models, data actions, currency conversions, and dimension tables all live in the file repository and follow the same handling paradigm. There is no longer a separate mental model for dimensions — everything works the same way.

Greatly Simplified Privilege Maintenance

Managing access to dimensions has historically been one of the more painful aspects of SAP Analytics Cloud administration. The new approach eliminates that friction in several important ways.

  • There is no longer any need to maintain roles specifically to hide a dimension that contains sensitive data. File repository sharing handles this naturally.
  • When a new dimension is created in "My Files", safe defaults apply automatically: only the creator has full control, plus administrators with access to private user folders. No one else can see the new dimension until it is explicitly shared.
  • You can configure access on a folder once and then simply move dimensions into that folder — they automatically inherit the folder's privileges. This is a significant time saver for tenants with many dimensions and complex access requirements.
  • A long-standing pain point is also resolved: the role maintenance UI used to display every individual dimension, including hundreds or thousands of "Date" and "Version" dimensions. This made it nearly impossible to identify such specific dimensions and, in tenants with large numbers of dimensions, caused the UI to become slow due to missing lazy loading. Individual dimensions no longer appear in the role maintenance UI at all.

Promote private dimension tables to public dimension tables

You can now convert a private dimension table by saving it in the file repository as a public dimension table to benefit from folder-level access management, re-usability and access to data management capabilities.. 

 

What Changes in the User Interface

Public Dimensions in the File Repository

Public dimensions are now visible in the file repository browser alongside models, stories, and other artefacts. You can rename them, move them, update their descriptions, or transfer ownership — all without affecting any models that reference them.

Sharing Instead of Role Maintenance

To change privileges for a dimension or grant access to other users or teams, you now use the standard file repository sharing functionality. Individual dimensions no longer appear in the role maintenance UI.

Public Dimension Tables Landing Page

The public dimension table landing page no longer loads and displays all public dimensions on open. Instead, it shows the 25 most recently used public dimension tables — consistent with the behavior already in place for models and currency conversions.

Saving a New Public Dimension

When saving a new public dimension, the file repository save dialog appears, allowing you to specify the parent folder, the name, and a description — exactly as you would when saving any other file repository artifact.

Save as Public Dimension

Convert a private dimension table to a public dimension table to benefit from the previously mentioned benefits of folder-level access management, re-usability and access to data management capabilities.

Creating Dimensions Inside a Model

It is no longer possible to choose "Make Public" when adding a new dimension directly inside a model. The alternatives are:

  • Create public dimensions upfront before creating the model and reference them when building the model.
  • Create a new private dimension, save the model, then use "Save As Public Dimension" to create a copy and "Dimension Table > Replace" to swap the Private dimension for the newly saved public one.

New Create Dimension Dialog

The "Create Dimension" dialog has been redesigned. It now includes a file repository browser for selecting existing public dimensions of a given type — Account, Organization, or Generic. In seamless planning models, it only allows picking dimensions saved in the same SAP Datasphere space as the model.

Replace Dimension Dialog

It now includes the same file repository browser for selecting existing public dimensions.

Private Dimension Tables

Private dimension tables do not appear in the file repository browser. However, access management has moved away from roles and uses file repository functionality:

  • You now control who can see and edit a private dimension using the same file repository sharing mechanisms available for all other artifacts, reachable directly from the model or dimension editor. Sharing a model with a user or team automatically shares all private dimensions of that model with the same privileges.
  • It is also possible to restrict access to individual private dimensions further than the model itself.

 

Central Migration: What Happens to Your Existing Dimension Tables

The migration to the file repository runs automatically as part of the QRC3.2026 upgrade on QRC and preview tenants or as part of the wave 2026.11 upgrade on fast-track tenants. Here is what you need to know.

What Gets Migrated

  • All public dimension tables receive a file repository artefact.
  • These artefacts are created in a new folder: Public > Dimension Tables.
  • Private dimensions are technically migrated as well but they will not be visible in the file repository.

What does not change

Note that the migration does not change the dimension tables itself. IDs and content stay the same. No effects on the consumption in models, stories, data actions etc. are to be expected.

How Existing Role Assignments Are Handled

The migration applies the same algorithm used for earlier file repository migrations such as Data Actions, Multi Actions and Currency Conversions.

  • Teams following the naming convention "DimensionRole_<role name>" are generated for roles where at least two users have that role directly assigned. Those users are added to the generated team, and the relevant dimensions are shared to that team. For roles with single user or only team(s), the permissions will be attached to the individual or team(s).
  • If the tenant already has all users organized in teams with roles assigned to those teams, no additional teams will be generated — existing teams are reused.

Privilege gaps after migration

Due to the way file repository privileges work, some users may find they are missing access to dimensions after the migration.

  • Before the migration, a create-only role was sufficient — users automatically had full control over any dimension they created as the owner. The file repository does not work this way. To achieve the same level of control as before, users need a role that explicitly grants Read, Delete, Update, and Maintain privileges.

  • After the migration, new users will not automatically receive read access to local dimensions of models that have not been explicitly shared with them — for example, when their model access comes from folder authorizations rather than a direct share. This is by design: local dimensions are not shared with read privilege to "All Users," because some customers intentionally apply stricter access controls at the dimension level than at the model level.

We recommend reviewing your role assignments after the migration and updating them accordingly.

Import of Transport Packages

When importing transport packages created before the migration, dimension tables are automatically saved/migrated to the file repository.

Bookmarks to Public Dimension Tables

Browser bookmarks created to directly access public dimension tables will cease to work after the migration. We need to add a prefix for all public dimension tables to avoid name collisions with other objects.

Behavior During the Migration Window

While the migration is running, creating and deleting dimensions is blocked and will result in backend errors. Depending on the number of dimensions and roles in your tenant, this could take several hours.

The migration runs right after the upgrade of your tenant to QRC3/wave 2026.11. This means users can be logged in while the migration is in progress and may encounter these backend errors during that window.

 

Summary

The move of dimension tables to the file repository is a significant step forward. It completes the unification of all object types under a single, consistent artefact management model, eliminates long-standing pain points in role maintenance, and gives administrators a much more intuitive and scalable way to control access to sensitive data. We recommend reviewing your role assignments after the migration and updating them accordingly.

entry.title: Improved Data Access in Stories: Introducing the Data Panel and Drag & Drop Creation of Charts
entry.content:
The BDC SAP Analytics Cloud Team is constantly looking for ways to make data storytelling faster, more intuitive, and more accessible. With the 2026 Q2 QRC, we are excited to introduce a new foundation and set of improvements that fundamentally rethink how you discover, access, and work with data in SAP Analytics Cloud Stories.

The Data Panel: Your Starting Point for Self-Service Analytics

The Data Panel is a brand-new feature that builds on top of our Available Object List concept, which was introduced with the Optimized Experience. While the Available Object List was a first step, we recognized that it did not fulfill its potential as it always required you to select an existing object and launch via the Builder Panel. With this in mind, we've made significant improvements, not only to decouple the experience, but to meaningfully improve overall usability.

Transparent Data Access

In the past, consumers found it difficult to figure out how to add a data model to a Story. Most of the time, consumers would add an existing object which limited them to existing models or datasets, without realizing there was an additional entry point in the toolbar that provided access to data sources or to upload a file.

We are proud to announce that friction is now gone. Immediately when you create a new story, the Data Panel is surfaced right at the forefront, improving self-service workflows from the very first moment.

The Data Panel makes it immediately clear that you can add data from:

  • An Existing Model or Dataset
  • A Data Source
  • Upload a File

 

Recents & Favourites in Model Selection: Find Your Data Faster

We've recognized that consumers who consistently build insights from the same set of data models are frustrated by having to search for the same model again and again.

Now, once you select an existing model or dataset, you have access to:

  • Recents: data models you have actively used in the past, surfaced immediately so you can reuse them with a single click
  • Favourites: models you've explicitly starred for quick access

This dramatically reduces the number of clicks required to introduce a new data model into your story, especially for consumers who work repeatedly with the same datasets. Rather than browsing through folder structures or relying on search, your most relevant models are always a click away.

ⓘ it is an improvement that is available across SAP Analytics Cloud, such as Open a Theme, Save As a Story, and so on

 

Central Overview of Data

Once a data model has been added, the Data Panel becomes your central reference point for understanding and working with your data. Rather than navigating away from your Story, you can explore the full structure of the model directly within the panel:

  • Primary Structure (Account or Measure as it depends on your model)
  • Secondary Structure (Measure or Structure as it depends on your model)
  • Dimensions
  • Location Dimensions

This makes it significantly easier for consumers to understand the overall structure of their data model as they build additional visualizations, all without leaving the story canvas.

 

Continuous Exploration Across Data Models

It was very important to also enable continuous exploration. Consumers can now add additional data models directly within the Data Panel, and as each new model is added it is surfaced immediately, making it easier to navigate across multiple data models within the same story.

For complex dashboards, the Data Panel goes even further. As a consumer selects a specific visualization on the canvas, the Data Panel dynamically updates to reflect the data model associated with that visualization. If the consumer clicks away, the panel retains the last used data model, ready for the next visualization to be created.

For Blended Visualizations, selecting the visualization will invoke the full 'All Data' workflow, clearly highlighting via a blue indicator which models are in use, so consumers always have clarity on the data sources driving their analysis.

More Usability Improvements!

We also addressed a number of usability requests that customers have raised in the past:

  • Toggle between Description, ID, and ID & Description in the Data Panel making it easy to switch how entities are displayed 
  • Resize the Data Panel to suit your screen and content needs
  • Open in Modeller or Data Analyzer: under the three-dot menu, consumers can now open the current data model in a new tab, either in the Modeller or the Data Analyzer for further exploration. 

Decoupled from the Builder Panel

One of the most significant changes is the decoupling of the Data Panel from the Builder Panel. In the past, the Available Object List was always invoked through the Builder Panel.

Now, a lightweight toolbar on the right has been introduced, making it easier to:

  • Access or collapse the Data Panel independently
  • Access the Styling Panel or Builder Panel directly, without having to first open the Designer Panel
  • Collapse or Expand all right panels at once with a single action

This separation means the Data Panel can stand on its own as the primary workspace for data-first workflows, with the Builder Panel available as a manual configuration tool rather than the default starting point.

Powerful Search with Number of Entities Preview

For stories with large or complex data models, finding the right entity quickly is essential. As soon as a consumer invokes search within the Data Panel, a preview of matching entities is shown in real time, something that was not possible before.

For more complex data models, such as Universal Account Models with Multiple Hierarchies, the search experience goes even further. Matching entities are shown not only in the main panel list, but also within the account hierarchy dropdown menus, ensuring consumers can locate the exact field they need regardless of where it sits in the model structure.

Drag & Drop for Charts: Build Visualizations Naturally

With the Data Panel now surfaced alongside your story canvas, we've also improved drag and drop chart creation. The combination of the two makes this capability significantly more powerful.

Consumers now have two supported drag and drop workflows:

  • Drag from the Data Panel into the Builder Panel: the existing workflow, still fully supported
  • Drag from the Data Panel directly onto a Chart Widget on the canvas

Because the Data Panel is now decoupled, consumers can collapse the Builder Panel entirely and continue to build and modify charts purely through drag and drop, reducing panel clutter and keeping the focus on the canvas.

Updating Existing Visualizations

Previously, changing the data model on an existing visualization required the consumer to first clear the data source manually, which was a multi-click process. Now, consumers can simply drag a field from a different data model in the Data Panel directly onto an existing chart or into the Builder Panel, and the visualization will dynamically update to the new data model.

Where relevant, a confirmation prompt will appear to let the consumer know that the drag and drop action will cause a reset of the current chart configuration, keeping consumers informed while still dramatically reducing the number of steps required.

 

Building the Foundation for Data-First Workflows

With the introduction of the Data Panel, we've built the foundation for more advanced self-service use cases. The goal is to reduce the dependency on the Builder Panel as the primary interface, and instead establish a data-first workflow where consumers start with their data, explore its structure, and build visualizations naturally. The Builder Panel remains available for manual configuration, but it is no longer the gatekeeping step it once was.

These improvements, the Data Panel, Recents & Favourites, and enhanced Drag & Drop, are not isolated changes. Together they represent a deliberate shift toward a more intuitive, flexible, and powerful authoring experience in SAP Analytics Cloud Stories.

We're looking forward to hearing your feedback as you explore these features in the 2026 Q2 QRC. Keep a lookout on the Roadmap Explorer to see when we extend this to Tables and allow Drag & Drop directly to the canvas!

entry.title: How To Trigger Automated Email Notifications Based on KPI Thresholds in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

In many business scenarios, dashboards are expected to do more than just display KPIs. Business users also expect proactive alerts whenever critical numbers cross a certain threshold so that immediate action can be taken.

Recently, I worked on a requirement in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) where users needed to receive automatic notifications whenever a KPI exceeded a predefined limit. The alert had to be sent both as  an SAC notification and as an email. Another important requirement was that the process should run automatically on a specific day of the month without users having to open the dashboard manually.

In this blog, I’ll walk through the approach that was implemented using SAC scripting APIs such as getResultSet() and sendNotification() along with scheduled story publication.

Business Requirement

The requirement was straightforward but very useful from a business monitoring perspective.

The business wanted to:

  •             Monitor a KPI available in an SAC dashboard
  •             Compare the KPI value against a defined threshold
  •             Trigger an alert whenever the threshold condition is met
  •             Send email notifications to a predefined group of users
  •             Deliver alerts through: SAC notifications ,  Email notifications       
  •             Automate the entire process so that alerts are triggered on a scheduled date even if  nobody opens the dashboard

For example, if a KPI value becomes greater than 1000, users should automatically receive an alert.

Solution Overview

The solution was implemented using SAC Application Designer / Optimized Story scripting capabilities.

The main logic was written inside the On Initialization event of the story. Whenever the story initializes, the script reads the KPI value directly from the chart using the getResultSet() API.

The retrieved value is then converted into a numeric format and compared against the threshold value. If the condition is satisfied, the sendNotification() API triggers both SAC notifications and email alerts for the configured users.

To automate the process, the story publication was scheduled monthly to developers ID. During the scheduled execution, the story initializes automatically, which in turn executes the notification logic without requiring manual dashboard access.

High-Level Process Flow

The overall process works as follows:

  1.           The SAC story/dashboard initializes
  2.           The On Initialization script gets triggered
  3.           The script reads the KPI value from the chart using getResultSet()
  4.           The KPI value is converted into numeric format
  5.           The value is validated against the threshold
  6.           If the condition is met, a notification is prepared
  7.           SAC notification and email are sent to the configured users
  8.           Story publication scheduling automates the execution on the required date

During scheduled execution, the script runs automatically and sends alerts if needed.

Prerequisite

Before implementing this solution, ensure that email notifications are enabled in SAP Analytics Cloud.

 

Technical Implementation

1. Fetching KPI Value from Chart

The KPI value can be accessed using the getResultSet() API available on charts and tables.

 Below is the logic used to fetch the KPI value:

 var check_data = Chart_1.getDataSource().getResultSet()[0][Alias.MeasureDimension].rawValue;       This retrieves the raw KPI value from the chart result set.

2. Formatting the KPI Value

The returned value may sometimes be in string format, so it is important to convert it into a numeric value before comparison.

var value = Number.parseInt(check_data);

3. Threshold Validation

Once the value is converted, it can be validated using a simple conditional statement.

var notificationContent = ""; 
var title = ""; 
if(value > 1000){ 
    title = "Alert - The value is greater than 1000"; 
    notificationContent = "Dear User, Please be informed that the value is    greater than 1000"; 
} 
else{ 
    title = "Key figures look good";
    notificationContent = "Key figures look good"; 
}

4.  Sending Notifications

The sendNotification() API is used to send alerts to specific SAC users.

Application.sendNotification(
     {
        title: title,
        content: notificationContent,
        receivers: ["user_id"], 
        mode: ApplicationMode.Present,
        isSendEmail: true 
     });

This sends:

  •             An SAC notification
  •             An email notification (if email settings are enabled)

Complete Script

var check_data = Chart_1.getDataSource().getResultSet()[0][Alias.MeasureDimension].rawValue;
 console.log(check_data); 
var value = Number.parseInt(check_data); 
var notificationContent = ""; 
var title = ""; 
if(value > 1000){
  title = "Alert - The value is greater than 1000"; 
  notificationContent = "Dear User, Please be informed that the value is greater than 1000"; 
} 
else{
  title = "Key figures look good";
  notificationContent = "Key figures look good"; 
}
 Application.sendNotification(
  { 
    title: title,
    content: notificationContent,
    receivers: ["user_id"],
    mode: ApplicationMode.Present,
    isSendEmail: true 
  });

Automating Alerts Using Scheduled Publication

Initially, notifications were getting triggered only when users opened the dashboard manually.

However, the requirement was to run the alerting process automatically on a specific day every month.

To achieve this, the SAC story publication feature was used.

The story was scheduled for publication to a specific user on the required date. During the scheduled execution, the story automatically initialized in the background, which triggered the On Initialization script.

As a result:

  •             KPI validation happened automatically
  •             Threshold conditions were checked
  •             Notifications and emails were sent automatically whenever the condition was met

This approach helped in building a simple but effective automated KPI monitoring mechanism inside SAP Analytics Cloud without requiring any external integration or custom scheduling tools.

OUTPUT EMAIL :

Final Thoughts

This solution is very useful for proactive business monitoring scenarios where users need immediate visibility into KPI fluctuations.

Using SAC scripting APIs along with scheduled publication makes it possible to build lightweight alerting mechanisms directly within SAP Analytics Cloud.

Some additional enhancements that can be explored:

  •             Dynamic threshold configuration
  •             Multiple KPI monitoring
  •             Role-based recipient management
  •             Integration with external notification channels
  •             Exception dashboards for alert tracking

I hope this blog helps anyone looking to implement automated KPI-based alerts in SAC.

If you are here, thank you so much for taking out your time to read this. Your feedback will be highly appreciated.

Thanks,
Himanshu

entry.title: Live Versions for Planning Models in SAP Analytics Cloud: Microsoft Azure Is Here!
entry.content:
Introduction

The live data access story for SAP Analytics Cloud planning models keeps getting stronger. When we introduced live versions in QRC4 2025, we opened the door for planning models to connect directly to live SQL sources without replication. Google BigQuery was the first supported source, followed by Snowflake in QRC1 2026. With this release, Microsoft Azure joins the portfolio — and the pattern should by now be a familiar one.

If you missed the earlier posts in this series, here is a quick recap: live versions allow a planning model in SAP Analytics Cloud to reference fact data directly from an external source at query time, without importing or replicating that data into SAC. The live data is read-only — you cannot write back to the source — but you can use it in calculations, data actions, and stories, and copy it into planning versions when needed. This applies to SAC models that are not deployed to SAP Datasphere. For seamless planning scenarios, live connectivity to SAP Datasphere is the way to go. Learn more:

  • Live Versions from an SQL Source for SAP Analytics Cloud Planning
  • SAP Analytics Cloud Planning with Live Snowflake Data
  • Unlocking the Next Chapter of Seamless Planning in SAP Business Data Cloud with Live Versions

With Microsoft Azure now supported, customers running their data on Azure can bring that data directly into their SAC planning models without any replication pipeline. Let's walk through how it works.

 

How Does It Work?

There are two ways to get started with live versions from Microsoft Azure: you can create a new model directly from your Azure data, or add a live version to an existing model. We will cover both approaches.

Creating a New Model from Azure Data

Alternatively, you can create a planning model directly from your Azure data source — a clean approach when starting from scratch. The video example is based on Snowflake. It is the same workflow, just choose Azure instead.

  • Go to the Modeler and select Create Model
  • Choose Create Model from Data
  • Select Microsoft Azure — you can filter the connection list by Data Storage "Cloud" to find it quickly
  • Choose your connection and select a view
  • The model is created along the structure of the source view, with a live connection established immediately — visible in the data preview

From here, you work with the model as you would with any other SAC planning model. You can remove dimensions and measures you do not need, define calculations directly on the live data, and use data actions to copy actuals from Azure into a plan version stored in SAC's own fact table.

Working with the model

 

Use live version data in planning

Adding a Live Version to an Existing Model

Open your model and navigate to the Data section in the model details. You will find a new entry called External Live Version Data Sources.

From there, the steps are straightforward. The video example is based on Google BigQuery. It is the same workflow, just choose Azure instead.

  • Select Connect External Data Source
  • Choose an existing target version or create a blank one
  • Select Microsoft Azure as the source system
  • Choose your connection
  • Select your data source from Azure
  • Map the data source columns to your model columns — not every source or target column needs to be mapped, measures can be NULL, and dimensions can receive a default member
  • Review any performance hints or master data mismatch notifications — missing dimension members can be added directly from this dialog
  • Start using the live data in calculations, data actions, and stories

Add a live version to an existing model

You can add multiple live sources to a single model if needed.

 

FAQ

Where can I use this feature?

This feature is available for SAP Analytics Cloud models in SAP Analytics Cloud tenants running on HANA Cloud that are not deployed to SAP Datasphere (i.e. not using seamless planning).

Can I use any source view from Azure?

Yes, with the caveat that not all data types are supported. Please refer to the product documentation for the full list of supported data types.

What can I do with live versions — and what are the limitations?

You can:

  • Display live version data in the model data foundation and in tables, charts, and other widgets in stories
  • Reference live version data in model and story calculations as well as data actions, including advanced formulas

You cannot:

  • Write back to the Azure source directly. To persist planned data, copy the live version data into a planning version via copy/paste in the table, data actions, or version management — the plan data is then stored in the SAC fact table

How do I know if data is live or replicated?

In the model data preview, live versions are highlighted and the data source is shown per version in the version dimension. In stories, live version data is locked for data entry and formatted distinctly. Unplannable data reasons provide accurate context.

Conclusion

With Microsoft Azure joining Google BigQuery and Snowflake as a supported live source, SAP Analytics Cloud continues to expand its openness to the data platforms customers already use. Whether you are running your fact data in Azure, Snowflake, or BigQuery, you can now bring it directly into your planning model without any replication overhead — and without giving up the full power of SAC planning, calculations, and stories.

This is part of SAP Analytics Cloud's broader alignment with SAP Business Data Cloud's ambition to be a truly open platform. Follow the Roadmap Explorer to stay up to date on further additions to the live connectivity portfolio. Live connectivity to Microsoft SharePoint Excel files and Microsoft SharePoint Lists is currently planned for QRC4.2026!

Have questions or feedback? Drop them in the comments below.

entry.title: Driving the Future of Water: SAP Brings Together Water Industry Leaders in Madrid
entry.content:
The water sector is at a transformative crossroad. Utilities and public service organizations worldwide are navigating increasing pressure to modernize operations, strengthen resilience, and deliver smarter, more sustainable services to communities. To meet this moment, SAP is organizing the Executive Value Network (EVN) for Water & Wastewater, next October 21-22 in Madrid as an international knowledge sharing oriented forum for those interested in digital transformation best practices of Water and Wastewater companies.

An Event Built on Strong Partnerships

Hosted by Canal de Isabel II, the Water Company of Madrid  and one of Europe’s most recognized water utilities, it will provide the ideal setting for a gathering of this significance. Farys, the Gent based Belgian water, sewage and municipal services company, will as a strategic advisor, contributing deep sector expertise and a collaborative vision for the future.

Complementing a strong ecosystem of support, Minsait and SEW – Smart Energy and Water join as partners, bringing complementary capabilities in digital transformation, technology integration, and water sector innovation. Together, this network of hosts and partners reflects the very spirit of the event: that progress in the water industry is best achieved through collaboration.

Two Keynotes Not to Be Missed: Canal de Isabel II and ANDA

At the heart of the event program will be two exceptional keynote sessions, each telling a compelling and distinct story about digital transformation in the water sector across very different contexts. Together, they demonstrate that while every utility’s journey is unique, the common purpose is universal: delivering better, more sustainable water services to people and communities.

Representing the host utility, María González Cano from Canal de Isabel II will open with a keynote that offers a mature, large-scale example of how to embed digital capabilities into the very heart of water operations and governance. As one of Europe’s leading metropolitan water utilities, Canal de Isabel II’s keynote sets the tone for the entire event — illustrating what is possible when digital ambition meets operational discipline.

Dagoberto Arévalo, CEO, and Dennis Barrientos, CIO of the Administración Nacional de Acueductos y Alcantarillados (ANDA) from El Salvador will bring a different, but equally powerful perspective to the stage. As a national water authority in an emerging economy, ANDA’s Digital Transformation Journey story is one of determination, pragmatism, and impact — demonstrating that digital transformation is a global imperative, not the exclusive domain of large European utilities by executing their 2025-2030 strategic plan.

 

A Rich Agenda: Three Focused Tracks for Every Stage of the Journey

Beyond the keynotes, the event agenda is structured around three main tracks, designed to help participants move from strategic vision to concrete action, regardless of where they are on their digital journey.

 

Strategic Asset Management: From a Technical Approach to a Citizen-Centric Perspective

Sessions will explore how to build a single source of truth for assets by bringing together technical, financial, operational, and service data; how to apply predictive analytics and risk-based planning to prioritize investments where they matter most for customers and communities; and how to connect asset strategies to resilience, sustainability, and quality of service.

The key shift this track champions is simple but powerful: start from citizens’ needs and work backwards into technology and operations — not the other way around.

This track will include an inspirational session which tackles one of the most fundamental questions in water management: how do you plan and invest in infrastructure so that real people receive reliable, safe, and affordable water services?

Maria Gonzalez (Canal Isabel II) and Inge Opreel (Farys)

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Smart Metering

The smart metering track examines how digital measurement and connectivity can transform both operations and customer experience. Topics include architectures for Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) and their integration with SAP back-end systems, turning meter data into actionable insights for leak detection and demand forecasting, enabling new tariff models based on high-frequency consumption data, and managing organizational change as billing, field operations, and customer care become increasingly data-centric.

Sovereign Cloud Strategies

This track addresses one of the most strategic questions facing utilities today: how to embrace cloud innovation while ensuring data protection, compliance, and control. Sessions will cover the concept of sovereign cloud and why it matters for public and critical infrastructure operators, SAP’s approach to supporting data residency, security, and regulatory requirements, and hybrid migration strategies that allow utilities to innovate without disrupting mission-critical operations.

 

Research and Innovation: A Window into What Comes Next

Complementing the three main tracks, the agenda also features a dedicated Research and Innovation stream — designed for those who want to look beyond today’s best practices and anticipate tomorrow’s challenges.

This stream will showcase R&D projects, pilots, and collaborations between utilities, universities, startups, and technology partners. Topics will include AI and machine learning for water operations, digital twins, real-time water quality monitoring, and circular economy initiatives. It is a unique opportunity to discover new ideas and connect with innovators who are already shaping the next generation of water management

 

Networking, Dinner, and Hands-On Experiences

Great events are not only built in the conference room. This program has been designed to foster informal exchange, real-world learning, and lasting connections beyond the formal sessions.

Joint Dinner Sponsored by Minsait: On the evening of the first day, Minsait will host a joint dinner for all participants — a chance to continue the day’s conversations in a more relaxed and convivial atmosphere, explore potential collaborations, and build the kind of relationships that make a real difference back home.

Site Visit on Day Two: Control Center (CPC) and Metrology Laboratory: The second day features an exclusive site visit organized by Canal de Isabel II, offering participants a behind-the-scenes look at how digital strategies translate into real operations, this is connecting what is discussed in the room with what is delivered in the field.

  • Control Center (CPC): See how the Madrid water system is monitored and managed in real time — including how operational data, alarms, and analytics support decision-making and incident response across one of Europe’s largest urban water networks.
  • Metrology Laboratory: Discover how metering devices are tested, calibrated, and certified, and how robust metrology practices underpin reliable billing, smart metering projects, and overall data quality.

 

 

 

 

 

 



Whether you are a utility executive, a digital transformation leader, or a technology innovator, this event offers a unique opportunity to connect with peers, learn from world-class organizations like Canal de Isabel II and ANDA, and explore how SAP’s solutions can help your organization take its next step forward.

We look forward to welcoming you to Madrid.

To register or learn more, visit: https://events.sap.com/evn-water2026/en_us/home.html

entry.title: Handling Blank Values in BW Data Blending and Time Dimension Filters in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

While working with blended SAP BW data in SAP Analytics Cloud, I encountered an issue where some costs were missing from reports. After investigation, the root cause was related to blank date values and the way SAP Analytics Cloud handles time dimension filters.

Issue Description

When blending BW data in SAP Analytics Cloud, blank values are displayed as "#" in reports.

If a dimension filter uses the "Filter by Members" option, there is no data quality issue because blank members are included and displayed as "#".

However, the issue occurs when a dimension filter uses the "Filter by Range" option. A common example is displaying a date dimension at Year level to avoid showing the full date format (DD.MM.YYYY).

In this scenario, SAP Analytics Cloud ignores records with blank date values. As a result, those records are excluded from the report, leading to missing costs and incomplete data.

Impact

The report may appear correct at first glance, but costs associated with records containing blank dates are not included.

This can lead to:

• Missing cost values

• Incomplete reporting

• Potential data quality concerns

Solution

A practical workaround is to use the "Filter by Members" option together with the "Year" hierarchy.

This approach:

• Preserves records with blank dates

• Prevents missing costs

• Maintains reporting at the Year level

One limitation is that blank date records cannot be identified directly through the Year hierarchy.

To identify records with blank dates, apply a filter on another dimension and select "#" (blank value).

Conclusion

When using blended BW data in SAP Analytics Cloud, special attention should be given to time dimension filters. Range-based filtering can unintentionally exclude records with blank dates, resulting in incomplete reporting. Using "Filter by Members" with the Year hierarchy provides a simple and effective workaround while maintaining data completeness.

entry.title: Improving SAP Analytics Cloud Story Performance with a Simple Landing Page
entry.content:
Introduction

Performance issues can negatively impact user experience in SAP Analytics Cloud, especially when stories load large volumes of data immediately upon opening.

In one of our reporting stories, users frequently experienced long loading times, system hanging, and occasional crashes. A simple design change significantly improved the user experience.

Issue

The story contained multiple tables and charts that loaded data at the all products level as soon as the story opened.

As a result:

• Large amounts of data were retrieved immediately.

• Story opening time increased significantly.

• Users experienced system hanging and slow response.

• In some cases, the story became unresponsive.

Solution

Instead of opening the story directly on a data-intensive page, I created a blank landing page without any tables, charts, or other data-consuming widgets.

The landing page contains only filter controls and an instruction message for users.

Message Displayed to Users

"Welcome to the story.

This first page is kept intentionally blank to improve performance and avoid heavy data loading.

Please select and update all required filters first.

After setting the filters, go to the next page to view the data.

This helps reduce loading time and avoids system hanging or crashes."

Benefits

The solution provided several benefits:

• Reduced initial data loading.

• Faster story opening time.

• Lower system resource consumption.

• Improved user experience.

• Reduced complaints about hanging and crashes.

• Increased user satisfaction.

Why This Works

When users first apply the required filters, the amount of data retrieved on subsequent pages is significantly reduced.

Instead of loading all available data at story startup, the system loads only the filtered data required by the user.

This simple approach improves performance without modifying BW queries, models, or backend systems.

Conclusion

Performance improvements do not always require complex technical changes. Sometimes a simple design enhancement can make a significant difference.

Creating a lightweight landing page that encourages users to set filters before viewing data helped reduce loading issues and improved overall user satisfaction in SAP Analytics Cloud.

entry.title: From “AI in Applications” to “AI on Applications”
entry.content:
If you work hands‑on with SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC), you’ve likely noticed that AI strategy announcements and new platforms often introduce ambitious ideas and evolving narratives. It’s common to look for clear connections to how these changes influence modeling, governance, integration, and consumption of data in practice. With SAP’s Autonomous Enterprise strategy and the announcement of the SAP Business AI Platform (BAIP) at Sapphire 2026, many data professionals are asking whether this represents a real architectural shift or simply a reframing of existing capabilities.

This post is written for practitioners already familiar with SAP Datasphere, SAP BW, SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP HANA Cloud, and SAP Master Data Governance, and focuses on what this strategy means in technical and operational terms for data professionals working with BDC today. It also offers a completely new perspective to data professionals who have never worked with these technologies and are intrigued by SAP’s Autonomous Enterprise vision.

What is not changing

Before discussing what’s new, it’s important to establish what remains true.

The Autonomous Enterprise doesn’t imply unattended automation or a loss of human supervision. AI agents operate within boundaries defined by data models, semantics, authorizations, and governance policies. Human intent, stewardship, and architectural decisions remain foundational. BDC is also not being replaced. BAIP doesn’t sit alongside BDC as a competing data platform. It builds on BDC and depends on it. Without harmonized data, preserved business context, and governed semantics, the Autonomous Enterprise cannot function reliably.

If anything, this strategy reinforces that strong data foundations are not optional for AI, they are prerequisites.

Why SAP is changing the AI engagement model

Most enterprise AI to date has been embedded inside applications. Predictive scores, recommendations, or copilots are scoped to a single process or system, often with limited visibility across business domains. The Autonomous Enterprise introduces a different model: AI on applications. Instead of embedding AI inside each application, SAP is introducing an agent‑based engagement layer that operates across applications, workflows, and data domains. These agents can reason over business intent, traverse processes, and execute actions using governed enterprise data. This shift only works if AI can reliably understand business meaning at scale. That requirement is what makes BAIP and BDC inseparable.

BAIP from a data architecture perspective

From a technical standpoint, SAP Business AI Platform is not a single service or runtime. It is a composition of cloud services that bring together AI models, agents, orchestration, governance, and data access into a coherent platform. Within BAIP, BDC makes the core of “Contextualize and Reason” services along with SAP Knowledge Graph. Its role is to provide AI agents with access to data that already carries business semantics, lineage, and policy enforcement. This includes SAP‑managed data products, customer‑defined derived data products, and harmonized data across SAP and non‑SAP sources using zero‑copy and federated data access patterns.

With a deep business process expertise that no one can match, SAP systems contain the most critical business data, forming the center of gravity for data and AI applications. Just to illustrate the size of SAP’s metadata model, SAP Knowledge Graph for SAP S/4HANA alone is based on 452,000 ABAP tables, 80,000 CDS views, and 7.3 million fields. That said, AI agents do not reason over raw tables or loosely defined datasets. They reason over data products with defined meaning, relationships, and access rules. BDC is the system that supplies those constructs.

 SAP Business AI Platform

How this addresses real data practitioner challenges

For data practitioners, the relevance of the Autonomous Enterprise strategy becomes clear when mapped to long‑standing pain points. 

Data context loss across systems remains one of the most expensive problems in enterprise data landscapes. Even modern architectures often strip business meaning as data moves between systems. In the Autonomous Enterprise model, AI agents rely on BDC’s business data fabric to preserve object relationships, hierarchies, and semantics across SAP and non‑SAP environments.

Semantic inconsistency is another persistent issue. KPIs and dimensions drift as data is replicated and remodeled. Under BAIP, semantic definitions authored in BDC – through Datasphere models, BW objects, HANA calculation views, and governed master data – are reused not only for analytics, but also for AI‑driven reasoning and action.

Manual orchestration and governance overhead is the third major friction point. In the Autonomous Enterprise architecture, governance is enforced by design. AI agents discover and consume data through BDC services that already apply authorization policies, data product contracts, and stewardship rules. Governance becomes intrinsic to execution rather than an after‑the‑fact control.

How this maps to BDC components

At a practical level, the Autonomous Enterprise strategy doesn’t introduce a new data stack. It activates existing BDC components in a broader execution context.

  • SAP Datasphere continues to provide semantic modeling, data product definition, and cross‑system harmonization. Those models are no longer consumed only by dashboards and planners, but also by AI agents operating through BAIP.
  • SAP BW remains a governed source of enterprise semantics and historical business logic, with its models surfaced as data products that retain their meaning when consumed by analytics, planning, or AI.
  • SAP HANA Cloud contributes intelligent compute, advanced SQL, and increasingly AI‑aware capabilities that support both transactional and analytical data products used by agents and applications.
  • SAP Analytics Cloud remains the primary analytical and planning consumption layer, while also becoming a beneficiary of AI‑assisted insight generation grounded in the same governed data products.
  • SAP Master Data Governance provides the quality and consistency layer that ensures AI agents reason over accurate, harmonized master data rather than fragmented representations.

Together, these components form the trusted knowledge core that BAIP relies on to make agent‑based AI reliable and explainable.

The technical takeaway

The Autonomous Enterprise strategy doesn’t reduce the importance of data engineering, modeling, or governance. It raises the bar. Putting AI on applications only works when AI can rely on consistent semantics, preserved business context, and enforced governance. BDC provides that foundation. BAIP operationalizes it. For data professionals, this strategy is less about adopting a new platform and more about extending existing BDC practices into an AI‑first execution model.

Join the conversation

These changes will surface new architectural patterns, design trade‑offs, and best practices. The Autonomous Enterprise will not be built in a single release. It will be built incrementally on the data foundations practitioners are already creating today. Join the Data Professionals community discussions to exchange technical perspectives, share lessons learned, and explore how others are evolving their BDC foundations to support agent‑based AI.

entry.title: Conversational Analytics in SAP Joule with SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
In today’s digital landscape, intuitive insights and intelligent assistance are becoming essential for business users. SAP Joule, the AI-powered digital assistant from SAP, takes this a step further by enabling natural-language queries and actionable insights across enterprise applications. One of the most impactful capabilities of Joule is its ability to connect with SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) - bringing conversational analytics directly into business workflows.

This blog provides a simplified overview of how SAP Joule is integrated with SAP Analytics Cloud using SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP).

Prerequisites for Joule Onboarding

  • Entitlements for Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication and Identity Provisioning.
  • To support navigation to different applications from Joule and handle role-based access scenarios, you need to configure the navigation service of SAP Build Work Zone.

Note: By default, SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition uses the SAP Authentication and Trust Management service (XSUAA) for identity authentication. Therefore, we recommend first switching to SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Authentication as the authentication mechanism.

Required Applications/Services

  • Joule à Foundation
  • SAP Build Work Zone, Standard Edition à Foundation or Standard

Here are the steps for configuring Joule:

  1. Configure Destination to Use Navigation Service by creating a Navigation service destination
  2. Configure Trust to the Identity Authentication Tenant by adding the IAS URL in the joule subaccount trusted configuration
  3. Configure User Attributes for Joule from the Identity Directory
  4. Configure Trusted Domains in Identity Authentication
  5. Integration Process:

In the current release of Analytical Pattern in Joule, at least one existing Joule setup is required for any line of business (LoB), such as SAP SuccessFactors, SAP S/4HANA Cloud, or SAP Build Work Zone. Joule can also be used in stand-alone mode.

Here, we are using SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud System.

Since most SAP customers already have Joule activated, we address both configuration options below.

Option 1

  • Create a new SAP BTP Subaccount.
  • Run Joule booster

 

After completing the Joule Booster process, you will see the Joule formation in Ready status.

 

This completes the Joule activation for analytical insights. However, additional configuration steps are required.

Option 2

  • Within your SAP BTP Global Account, navigate to Formations, and search for the Joule formation you have already set up. You can click on Include System.

 

 

  • Review the included system. The system is now ready, which completes the Joule activation for analytical insights.

6. Post-integration checks

  • Once we follow the steps above, you can validate if the BTP Destination is created in your SAP BTP Subaccount. Destination named – “DYN_SAC_SSO”. Also, verify that the OAuth Client was created in your SAP Analytics Cloud system.

 

  • In your SAP Analytics Cloud, you should see a new line item for Joule in the SAC Roles. Ensure that the Read option is selected after the Joule Integration.

7. Create an OAuth Client for the Design Time in SAP Analytics Cloud

  • Log in to your SAP Analytics Cloud system and create OAuth client under the Configured Clients. 

 

 

  • Using this certificate, create a Trusted Identity Provider named Joule_IDP_Manual in SAC

8. Consume Role Definition Content from SAP Analytics Cloud

  • Log in to your SAP Analytics Cloud system and create an OAuth Client for Runtime in SAP Analytics Cloud.
  • Create an OAuth Client for Runtime in SAP Analytics Cloud
  • Note: Store the Secret value for DT and RT securely, as it will not be visible later.

9. Create a Design Time Destination in your SAP BTP Subaccount

After creating the destination, in additional Properties add the below properties.

HTML5.SetXForwardedHeaders: false &

URL.headers.x-sap-sac-custom-auth: true

10. Create a Runtime Destination by Cloning in our SAP BTP Subaccount

Select the existing DYN_SAC_SSO destination from BTP Joule subaccount and choose Duplicate to create a new destination based of it named (SAC_SWZ_RT)

11. Create a Content Provider in SAP Build Work Zone

To create a content provider in the SAP Build Work Zone, ensure you have the "Launchpad_Admin" role assigned.

 

12. Provision Users from SAP Analytics Cloud to SAP Build Work Zone Using IPS

Note: This process provisions users from SAP Analytics Cloud to SAP Build Work Zone. You will be working in SAP Cloud Identity Services and will need Administrator access.

 

  • Setup SAP SAC as a Source System in IAS
  • Setup SAP Build Work Zone as a Target System in IAS

 

 

13. Run User and Group Provisioning Job in IAS

              Check provisioning Logs to see the status.

14. Validate Provisioning

  • Open Build work zone application from BTP
  • Go to Channel Manager and refresh it to validate the provisioning.

15. Index Your Data Using Just Ask and Test Your Integration

The data available for your business users to ask Joule analytical questions is the data from supported models indexed by the just ask feature in your SAP Analytics Cloud tenant. Analytical insights support the following indexed models:

  • Models acquired from external sources and imported into SAP Analytics Cloud.
  • Analytic models from SAP Datasphere that are part of an SAP Business Data Cloud formation that also includes your SAP Analytics Cloud system.

Your users will need different permissions in SAP Analytics Cloud depending on their role:

  • To index models in the just ask feature of SAP Analytics Cloud, users will need the Maintain permission for the Just Ask privilege.
  • To ask questions to Joule's analytical insights feature and view answers and charts, users need the Read permission for the Joule privilege.

16. Open your S/4HANA Public Cloud instance and launch Joule

Query: - Give me a chart of discount per product Coca-Cola and per store

Conclusion: -

The integration of SAP Joule with SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) represents a significant advancement in making business intelligence more accessible through natural language interactions. By leveraging SAP Business Technology Platform, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Cloud Identity Services, and SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, organizations can enable conversational analytics that empowers users to explore data, generate insights, and visualize results without requiring deep technical expertise.

Successful implementation requires careful configuration of authentication, trust relationships, destinations, OAuth clients, content providers, and user provisioning. Once integrated, Joule can seamlessly access indexed SAC models through the Just Ask capability, allowing users to retrieve analytical insights, charts, and business information through simple conversational queries.

This integration enhances user productivity, accelerates decision-making, and promotes self-service analytics by bringing AI-driven insights directly into daily business workflows. As organizations continue their digital transformation journey, SAP Joule and SAP Analytics Cloud together provide a powerful foundation for intelligent, data-driven enterprise operations.

entry.title: Need to Know - Beyond SAP Analytics Cloud AI and Using SAP Databricks in SAP Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
The Data and Analytics Landscape Is Changing — Are You Ready?

For years, SAP customers have faced a familiar challenge: rich, high-quality business data locked inside ERP and line-of-business systems, difficult to extract, expensive to replicate, and often arriving too late to drive meaningful decisions. The emergence of AI and machine learning has only sharpened that challenge. Building a reliable AI model demands not just data volume, but data quality, business context, and governance — the very things that traditional ETL-and-copy approaches erode.

This blog is a follow on from my previous blog Need to Know - Unlocking the Power of AI in SAP Analytics Cloud explaining how you can extend AI, machine learning and predictive from SAP Analytics Cloud using SAP Databricks that is part of SAP Business Data Cloud.

Here is a summary of what each section covers:

Part 1 — SAP Business Data Cloud: Explains what BDC is.

Part 2 — What Is Databricks: Covers what Databricks is.

Part 3 — SAP Databricks in BDC: Explains the embedded, SAP-managed nature of the integration and why it matters when creating machine learning or predictive models.

Part 4 — Why Databricks Beyond SAC: Where Databricks helps beyond the AI, machine learning and predictive inside SAP Analytics Cloud.

Part 5 — Real-World Use Cases for SAP Databricks in BDC: Some example use cases summarised.

Part 6 — SAP Databricks and BDC Example A walkthrough from subscribing to the BDC Intelligent Application, through Delta Sharing, model training in SAP Databricks, sharing results back to BDC, and surfacing in the results using Datasphere inside SAC reports.

 

Part 1 - What Is SAP Business Data Cloud?

Launched in early 2025, SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) is SAP's fully managed, cloud-native SaaS platform for data and analytics. It is not simply a rebranding of existing tools — it is a fundamental rearchitecting of how SAP customers unify, govern, and activate their data.

At its core, BDC brings together the capabilities that SAP customers have historically relied on — such as SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud— under a single, cohesive modern data platform.

BDC establishes what SAP calls a Business Data Fabric — an architecture that connects data from SAP S/4HANA, SuccessFactors, Ariba, and other line-of-business applications, as well as third-party sources through a set of key capabilities:

  • Data Products: Pre-built, curated datasets delivered by SAP that carry full business semantics and context. These are not raw tables — they are governed, business-ready data assets covering domains such as finance, supply chain, and HR. They are accessed via open protocols including Delta Sharing, meaning data remains in place and is shared securely without duplication.
  • Foundation Services: The behind-the-scenes engine that handles data ingestion, transformation, and orchestration. Foundation Services use Open Resource Discovery (ORD) to catalogue and expose Data Products across the platform, storing data in HANA Data Lake Files using the Delta Lake format.
  • Intelligent Applications: Pre-built, fully SAP-managed analytical applications — covering Finance Intelligence, People Intelligence, Supply Chain Intelligence, and Working Capital — that are ready to consume with a single-click installation. They are built on top of Data Products, Datasphere models, and SAP Analytics Cloud stories, and include pre-defined metrics, models, and stories/applications to use the data immediately.  Unlike traditional SAP business content, Intelligent Applications are lifecycle-managed by SAP, including data integration, updates, and ongoing support.
  • SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC): The presentation and analytics layer within BDC, supporting business intelligence, planning, and scenario simulation. SAC connects live to Datasphere models and surfaces insights to consumers immediately.
  • SAP Datasphere: The semantic modelling and data integration hub within BDC. Datasphere hosts custom data models, manages connections to on-premise and third-party sources, and provides the analytical layer that SAC consumes.
  • SAP BW Private Cloud Edition PCE: For organisations with existing BW investments, BDC supports lifting on-premise BW  systems to a Private Cloud Edition managed by SAP, enabling continuity while unlocking access and modernising BW data using the BW Data Product Generator into Business Data Cloud Data Products.
  • BDC Connect: A secure, zero-copy data sharing capability that allows SAP and partner systems to exchange data products in real time, without building or maintaining ETL pipelines.

 

There are some excellent blogs out there that already cover SAP Business Data Cloud in more detail including this excellent one here https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-business-data-cloud-faqs/ba-p/14022781

The point of this blog is not to explain SAP Business Data Cloud again, but to explore how SAP Business Data Cloud, combined with SAP Databricks, can help deliver further AI, machine learning and predictive use cases using a data platform of governed, semantically rich, and AI-ready data.

 

Part 2- What Is Databricks, and Why Does It Matter?

Databricks is an independent data and AI company founded in 2013 by the creators of Apache Spark — the open-source distributed computing engine that underpins much of the modern big data ecosystem. Since then, Databricks has grown into one of the most widely adopted platforms for data engineering, data science, and machine learning at enterprise scale.

What does Databricks give organisations?

  • Apache Spark at scale: Distributed data processing capable of handling petabyte-scale datasets efficiently, enabling data engineering teams to build robust ingestion, transformation, and enrichment pipelines.
  • Collaborative notebooks: Data scientists and engineers work in shared Python, Scala, R, or SQL notebooks — enabling rapid experimentation, reproducibility, and team collaboration. Spark pipelines, SQL analysis, and ML model development all happen in the same environment.
  • MLflow: Databricks' open-source ML lifecycle management tool tracks experiments, manages model versions, and facilitates model deployment. It gives organisations full visibility into how models were trained, what data they used, and how they perform over time.
  • Mosaic AI (formerly Databricks ML): A comprehensive suite of AI and ML capabilities including AutoML for automated model training, Feature Store for curating and sharing ML features across teams, Model Serving for real-time and batch inference endpoints, and Vector Search for retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) use cases.
  • Unity Catalog: Databricks' unified governance layer for data and AI assets — covering tables, models, notebooks, and Delta Sharing recipients — with fine-grained access controls and full lineage tracking across the entire data and AI lifecycle.
  • Delta Sharing: An open protocol for sharing live data across organisational and platform boundaries without copying it. Data remains in the source location; recipients query it securely via a token-based credential system.

Organisations across industries — financial services, manufacturing, retail, life sciences — use Databricks because it resolves a problem that few other platforms address simultaneously: enabling data engineers, data scientists, business analysts, and ML engineers to work together on the same governed data, in the same environment, without the friction of hand-offs and data movement.  Read more about Databricks here https://www.databricks.com/

 

Part 3 - SAP Databricks Inside SAP Business Data Cloud

Through a landmark partnership announced alongside the launch of BDC, SAP has embedded Databricks directly into Business Data Cloud as a fully SAP-managed component. This means that when you subscribe to BDC and enable SAP Databricks, you are not integrating an external tool — you are activating a native capability within the same governed environment.

Using SAP Databricks with SAP data, enables data scientists and engineers to gain access to the Databricks pro-code environment while secure in the knowledge that they have access to secure, governed and semantically rich SAP business data.

Key capabilities of SAP Databricks inside BDC:

  • Zero-copy data access to SAP-managed Data Products via Delta Sharing — no pipelines, no replication
  • Pro-code development environment: Python notebooks, Spark pipelines, SQL analysis, and ML model training
  • Feature Store: Curate and share ML features across teams and use cases
  • Model training and validation: Experiment tracking via MLflow with full lineage and reproducibility
  • Unity Catalog: Govern all data and model assets with fine-grained access control and lineage
  • Delta Share back to BDC: Publish inference results as derived Data Products for consumption in Datasphere and SAC

While SAP packages Databricks inside BDC in the form of SAP Databricks it’s worth pointing out that organisations with an existing implementation of enterprise Databricks can use this with BDC instead of using SAP Databricks.  For more information about the SAP Databricks components above then have a look at this excellent blog https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/sap-databricks-is-now-ga-get-the-most-of-it-skilling-yourself-with-mosaic/ba-p/14091351

 

Zero-Copy Delta Sharing: The Foundation of Everything

The most architecturally significant aspect of SAP Databricks in BDC is how data flows between the Business Data Fabric and the Databricks environment. Rather than exporting, copying, or replicating data, BDC uses Delta Sharing — the open protocol co-developed by Databricks — to share Data Products directly with the SAP Databricks environment without any duplication.

Why does this matter?

When organisations extract data from SAP systems and rebuild it in a separate analytics or AI platform, they inevitably lose something: the business context and semantics that SAP has carefully maintained — the meaning of a cost centre, the hierarchy of a material group, the relationship between an invoice and a payment term. By the time data engineers have rebuilt that context in a new environment, it is often incomplete, stale, or inconsistent.

Delta Sharing eliminates this problem. SAP Data Products — already curated, governed, and carrying full business semantics — are shared directly to SAP Databricks via the Delta Sharing protocol. The data scientist in Databricks is not working with an export; they are working with the live, governed, semantically annotated dataset that SAP manages. No pipelines to build. No copies to reconcile. No semantic context to reconstruct.

 

What this means for AI and machine learning

The quality of an AI or ML model is a direct function of the quality and richness of its training data. When data arrives in a modelling environment already carrying business semantics — accounts payable ageing, material lead times, workforce attrition signals, customer payment patterns — the data scientist spends less time cleaning and engineering features, and more time building models that actually reflect the business problem.

The bidirectional nature of the integration compounds this value. After a data scientist builds and deploys a predictive model in SAP Databricks, the inference results — cash flow forecasts, churn scores, demand signals — can be Delta Shared back to BDC, where they become derived Data Products. Those derived Data Products are then available for consumption in SAP Datasphere analytical models and SAP Analytics Cloud dashboards. The AI output is not stranded in a separate tool; it flows back into the business fabric where decision-makers can act on it.

 

Part 4- Why Databricks — Going Beyond What SAP Analytics Cloud Already Offers

This is a question that comes up consistently in customer conversations: SAP Analytics Cloud already includes AI, Machine Learning and Predictive functionality in the form of Smart Predict, Smart Insights, Predictive Planning and more that allow business users to build predictive models, identify drivers, and generate automated forecasts without writing a line of code.

So why would you need SAP Databricks as well?

The answer lies in the nature of the problem being solved, the scale of data involved, and the profile of the person doing the work.

SAP Analytics Cloud enables end users to use predictive functionality embedded inside the tool and inside the process intended for this - i.e. Predictive Planning for forecasting is a good example where the functionality enables the planner to produce forecasts without having to be a data scientist.  Also, SAC allows you to create more sophisticated predictive models in the area of Smart Predict.  Smart Predict is designed for business analysts who need to build a regression or classification model on structured, moderate-scale datasets — think payment likelihood scoring on a customer portfolio. It is low-code, business-user-friendly, and tightly integrated with SAC stories and planning workflows. It is excellent at what it does.

However, it was not built for the following scenarios:

  1. Large-scale, complex feature engineering Enterprise AI use cases often require combining dozens of data sources — ERP transactional data, external market signals, weather feeds, social indicators, third-party risk ratings — and engineering hundreds of features from them. SAC Smart Predict operates on pre-built datasets; SAP Databricks can access SAP data and more external non-SAP data used to enhance the features required in these models.
  2. Custom model architectures SAC Smart Predict offers a curated set of algorithms (classification, regression, time series) with limited hyperparameter control. Data scientists working on complex problems need the freedom to build, experiment, and validate custom architectures. SAP Databricks provides that freedom via Python, TensorFlow, PyTorch, XGBoost, and the full Mosaic AI suite.
  3. Real-time inference at scale SAP Databricks Model Serving deploys trained models that can be called in real time by transactional applications, planning systems, or operational dashboards. SAC Smart Predict only operates within the SAC environment.
  4. Operationalised ML with full lineage Running ML in production is not just about training a model — it is about managing drift, scheduling retraining, monitoring data quality, tracking lineage from raw data to prediction, and maintaining audit trails. SAP Databricks, with MLflow and Unity Catalog, provides a mature MLOps framework for this. SAC Smart Predict does not provide equivalent MLOps tooling.

In summary: SAP Analytics Cloud and its Smart capabilities remain the right choice for business analysts using embedded AI functionality and for building governed, business-user-accessible predictive models within SAC. SAP Databricks is the right environment for data scientists and data engineers building custom, complex, large-scale AI and ML solutions that need to operate at the speed and sophistication that modern enterprise AI demands. The two are complementary, not competing — and BDC brings them together in a single governed platform.

 

Part 5 - Real-World Use Cases for SAP Databricks in BDC

The combination of governed SAP data and Databricks' AI/ML capabilities opens up a wide range of use cases across every line of business. Here are a couple of examples:

Accounts Receivable Collections Optimisation (Finance/Credit)

Using AR ageing data products from SAP S/4HANA, combined with customer financial health signals and historical payment behaviour, SAP Databricks models can predict which invoices are at risk of late or non-payment with high precision. Collections teams can be automatically prioritised towards the highest-risk, highest-value accounts, reducing DSO (Days Sales Outstanding) and improving working capital without increasing headcount.

Workforce Composition Analysis and Talent Intelligence (CHRO)

HR leaders face mounting pressure around talent acquisition costs, skills shortages, and workforce engagement in a volatile labour market. Understanding the composition, capability, and trajectory of a workforce requires more than headcount reports.

BDC integrates with SAP SuccessFactors, providing zero-copy access to Data Products covering employee profiles, skills inventories, role descriptions, performance metrics, and open requisitions. SAP Databricks blends this internal HR data with external labour market signals — industry salary benchmarks, skills-demand trends, competitor hiring patterns — to build predictive models for attrition risk, time-to-hire, and skills gap analysis.
