These models enable CHROs to identify at-risk employees before they leave, prioritise upskilling investments based on projected skills demand, and build data-driven talent acquisition strategies. Results feed back into SAC gives HR business partners and line managers actionable, model-driven workforce insights.

 

Part 6 -  SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Databricks — A Step-by-Step Walkthrough

Let's take the Accounts Receivable Collections Optimisation (Finance/Credit) use case from above and using SAP data with SAP Databricks predict which invoices are at risk of late or non-payment.

Step 1: Subscribe to the BDC Intelligent Application

In the BDC Cockpit, the organisation subscribes to the BDC Intelligent Application package. This triggers the automatic provisioning of SAP-managed Data Products from the connected SAP S/4HANA system, including:

    • Accounts Receivable Ageing — open invoices by customer, due date, payment terms, and historical payment behaviour
    • Accounts Payable Obligations — open vendor invoices, payment run schedules, and outstanding commitments
    • General Ledger Cash Positions — current bank balances, intercompany positions, and cash pool structures
    • Sales Order Pipeline — open orders not yet invoiced, with expected delivery and billing dates

 

The above screenshot shows the BDC Cockpit and an example of an Intelligent Application about to be installed.

These Data Products are delivered with full SAP business semantics — cost centres, company codes, currencies, fiscal calendars — maintained and governed by SAP. No custom extraction or data pipeline is required.

Step 2: Initiate Delta Sharing to SAP Databricks

From the BDC Data Product Catalog, the data engineer selects the relevant Finance Data Products and initiates a Delta Sharing connection to the SAP Databricks environment. This creates a Databricks catalog that reflects the live BDC Data Products — the data is not copied; the Delta Sharing protocol makes the tables available directly within the Databricks Unity Catalog as external shares.

 

 

The above screenshot shows sharing a Data Product using the Delta Sharing functionality.

The data engineer provisions SAP Databricks as the Delta Sharing recipient. From this point, any updates to the underlying SAP data products — new invoices posted, payments cleared, new sales orders raised — are immediately available to the Databricks environment without any pipeline or batch job.

 

Step 3: Using the SAP Data to Train, Validate and running the Cash Forecasting Model

In a Databricks Notebook, the data engineer writes a Python pipeline to ingest and blend data from SAP Data Products and from external data, such as

  • Foreign exchange rate feeds (from a financial data provider API) to normalise multi-currency receivables into group currency
  • Customer credit scores and payment behaviour data (from a credit bureau) to adjust expected payment dates
  • Macroeconomic indicators — central bank interest rate decisions, PMI indices — to model the impact of economic conditions on customer payment cycles

 

The above screenshot shows an example notebook accessing the shared SAP data  product for the predictive model.

The blended dataset is registered in the Databricks Feature Store — creating reusable, versioned features (e.g., customer_30d_payment_velocity, weighted_ar_collection_probability, fx_adjusted_payable_balance) that can be shared across multiple models and teams.

The data scientist uses a Databricks Notebook and begins model development:

  1. Exploratory analysis: Using Databricks SQL, the data scientist queries the Feature Store to understand the distribution of payment timing, identify seasonal patterns in cash inflows, and detect anomalies in the AR ageing profile.
  2. Feature engineering: Time-series features are constructed — rolling averages of collection rates by customer segment, lagged payment indicators, treasury balance trends.
  3. Model training: Multiple forecasting algorithms are evaluated using MLflow experiment tracking
  4. Model validation: Walk-forward cross-validation across the last 24 months of historical data. 
  5. Model registration: The best-performing model is registered in the Unity Catalog Model Registry with full lineage linking it to the Feature Store features and training Data Products it was built on.

 

Step 4: Share Results Back to BDC

The inference results — the predicted days (late or on-time) invoices will be paid — are Delta Shared back to SAP Business Data Cloud, where they become a derived Data Product in the BDC catalog.

 

The above screenshot shows the results data being shared inside SAP Databricks with SAP BDC.

 

In SAP Datasphere, a data analyst creates an Analytical Model that joins the Databricks predictions Data Product with live SAP general ledger positions and more.

 

The above screenshot shows the data flow used to create the Analytical Model which includes the predictions (delay_prediction_dataset) from SAP Databricks joined with data from S/4HANA, SAP Business Warehouse and Google BigQuery.

 

In SAP Analytics Cloud, a finance analyst builds a dashboard that displays:

    • Predicted overdue amounts
    • Predicted overdue amounts by days late
    • Predicted overdue amounts per customer

The above screenshot shows an example dashboard showing the results from the predictive model sliced by different dimensions.

 

The Business Outcome

What was previously a three-day manual process becomes a continuously updated, AI-driven forecast that:

  • Reduces forecast preparation time from days to minutes
  • Improves forecast accuracy by incorporating external signals and machine learning to predict cashflow
  • Enables proactive decision-making — treasury can initiate early payment discount programmes, adjust working capital facilities, or hedge FX exposure based on predicted signals, not historical actuals
  • Maintains full SAP governance — the underlying data never left the SAP-governed environment, and every model prediction is traceable back to its source data

 

SAP Business Data Cloud represents a significant step forward in how organisations can govern and activate their most valuable data asset: the operational data inside SAP systems. The integration of SAP Databricks takes that foundation and extends it into the domain of enterprise AI — not as a separate project, but as a native, governed, fully integrated capability.

The combination resolves a tension that has held back SAP AI adoption for years: the need to choose between the richness of SAP data and the power of open, pro-code AI tooling. With SAP Databricks in BDC, you no longer have to choose. The data stays governed, the semantics stay intact, and your data scientists get the best AI/ML platform in the market to build on top of it.

If your organisation is beginning to explore what AI can do with your SAP data — or if you have been frustrated by the gap between your data quality ambitions and your current tooling — SAP Business Data Cloud with SAP Databricks is worth serious evaluation.

entry.title: The AI Isn't the Problem. The Data Underneath It Is.
entry.content:
Most AI projects stall in the same place. The pilot worked. The model performed. Leadership approved the next phase. And then, without a clean explanation, the path to production got complicated.

It's rarely the AI. It's almost always the data.

Two-thirds of business leaders say they don't fully trust their data. For organizations trying to move AI from experiment to enterprise capability, that's not a survey result - it's a structural problem. The gap between a convincing proof of concept and a scalable, reliable AI deployment is, in most cases, a data readiness gap.

This month, that gap gets a framework.

---

## What's coming

The Open Data Institute (ODI), SAP's independent research partner on the Interchange for Data and Enterprise AI (IDEA), is releasing A Framework for AI-Ready Enterprise Data in late June. It's a rigorous, vendor-agnostic treatment of the problem: built from a comparative analysis of 23 published frameworks from organizations including Gartner, McKinsey, EY, Accenture, and Google Cloud, validated through expert interviews with senior data leaders at NTT Data, Clorox, and ZEISS, and grounded in 14 years of ODI experience building independent, trustworthy data infrastructure.

The result is 21 concrete, actionable criteria across four pillars - dataset properties, metadata, surrounding infrastructure, and governance - each calibrated for the enterprise environment and the specific demands of agentic AI.

It's not a whitepaper with broad recommendations. It's a practical checklist.

---

## The shape of the problem

The framework defines AI-ready data as "data that is technically optimized for machine learning, high quality and adherent to standards, legally compliant, and responsibly collected."

What makes the enterprise version significantly harder is scale, interconnection, and the nature of how AI now interacts with data.

Enterprise data doesn't live in tidy, isolated repositories. A customer record exists in the CRM, billing, and logistics systems - each version slightly different, each updated independently. A product code means one thing in finance and something adjacent in supply chain. That fragmentation is manageable when humans navigate it; people apply judgment, know which system to trust for which question. AI agents don't do that. They use what they can access, and they use it with confidence.

The problem compounds with agentic AI - systems that don't just answer queries but independently search for data, write and run code to analyze it, take actions based on what they find, and generate new data as a byproduct. That interaction pattern introduces failure modes that traditional data pipelines don't surface. A schema changes silently. A data feed shifted. The agent keeps running, producing outputs that look authoritative but are built on a corrupted foundation. The ODI framework calls this a "data cascade" - and it's the failure mode that scales with your AI adoption, not against it.

For anyone following SAP's Autonomous Enterprise strategy and the Business AI Platform announced at SAPPHIRE 2026, this should sound familiar. The premise of AI agents reasoning across SAP and non-SAP systems - traversing business processes, executing actions, generating outputs - depends entirely on the data foundation underneath being consistent, governed, and trustworthy. BAIP operationalizes the vision. The ODI framework is an independent measure of whether your data is ready for it.

The framework's 21 criteria address this directly. They cover how datasets should be structured and documented, how master records should be governed to establish a single source of truth, how metadata needs to evolve from descriptive labels into a machine-readable semantic map of the business, and how access controls need to be enforced at the infrastructure level rather than through natural language policies that agents can misinterpret or users can bypass.

Across four pillars, the framework adds six criteria specific to enterprise - including master data management, semantic interlinkage and active cataloguing, monitoring and observability checkpoints, and agent-aware access controls via policy-as-code. These are not theoretical additions. They are the capabilities that separate organizations successfully scaling AI from those stuck cycling through pilots.

The full report is worth reading before it's public. It's a concrete, independently validated treatment - and it maps directly to the decisions experts building on SAP infrastructure are making right now.

If you've been through an AI pilot - what was the first thing that broke when you tried to scale it? The data, the governance, or something else?

---

## Get the report before it's public

The ODI report publishes at the end of June. IDEA membership is free - and includes early access to the report along with a peer community of senior data leaders working through exactly these questions.

Join IDEA and request early access to the report

---

IDEA - the Interchange for Data and Enterprise AI - is a programme of independent research and peer learning developed in partnership between SAP and the Open Data Institute. Nine roundtables. 140+ senior executives. Three research briefs. And a framework that's about to change how enterprise AI readiness gets measured.

entry.title: Use Formations to Link SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere for Seamless Planning
entry.content:
Introduction

If you are setting up seamless planning with SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere, there are two ways to link the tenants: via the Tenant Links tab in the SAC system administration, or via a formation in the SAP BTP cockpit or SAP for Me. Going forward, use the formation approach — and this post explains why, how, and what to do if you have already configured the link via the SAC admin UI.

 

Why Use Formations?

The formation-based setup is the recommended and more robust approach to linking SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere for seamless planning. Here is why it matters:

  • More stable setup: The formation approach results in a more reliable and consistent integration between the two tenants.
  • SAP-managed OAuth clients: OAuth clients created via formations are managed by SAP, reducing the risk of misconfiguration and improving security.
  • Future features: Some new capabilities — such as pushing data from SAP Datasphere into seamless planning models — may only be delivered for formation-based connections. The tenant administration UI will not receive these enhancements.
  • The tenant administration UI will be deprecated with SAP Analtics Cloud QRC3.2026. Created links will continue to work. Migrating is not mandatory but recommended for the reasons above. As outlined below, the migration respects all the seamless planning content you have already created.

 

How to Set Up the Formation

To link your SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere tenants via a formation – based on the BTP cockpit:

  1. In the SAP BTP cockpit, go to System Landscape > Formations and select Create Formation.
  2. Enter a descriptive name and select Integration with SAP Analytics Cloud as the formation type.
  3. Select the SAP Analytics Cloud tenant you want to link with.
  4. Select the SAP Datasphere tenant you want to link with.
  5. Review and confirm. A Ready status confirms the formation is active.

Prerequisite: Both tenants must be in the same data center and landscape, and must use the same identity provider (IdP) and subject name identifier. SAP Analytics Cloud must run on HANA Cloud.

 

Already Linked via SAC Tenant Administration? Here Is What to Do.

Migrating is recommended for the reasons outline above. It is not mandatory as of now.

If you have already configured data storage for planning via the Tenant Links tab in the SAC system administration, migration is straightforward: when you create a formation in SAP BTP cockpit using the same SAC and SAP Datasphere tenants that are already linked, the existing configuration is automatically migrated. All related artifacts remain intact. There is no need to redo the configuration manually. 

Important: Make sure to use the exact same tenants in the new formation that are already linked in your SAC tenant administration. Using different tenants will not trigger the migration and may result in a duplicate or conflicting configuration.

 

Learn More

  • For full setup instructions and prerequisites, refer to the official product documentation: Configure Data Storage for Planning
  • Seamless planning integration between SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere - product FAQ

entry.title: Can I Dynamically Color SAC Table Cells Based on Min, Mid, and Max Input Values
entry.content:
Hi,

I have 4 SAC tables created on SAC import models, and each table contains a fixed grid of 308 cells (14 columns × 22 rows). All of these cells will have numeric values populated either with 0 or higher.

I would like to add 3 input text boxes:

  1. Minimum (background color: #92D050 – Green)
  2. Midpoint (background color: #FFEB84 – Yellow)
  3. Maximum (background color: #C00000 – Red)

Users will enter values into these text boxes, and the entered values will define the color ranges that should be applied across all 4 tables.

My goal is to write a script that iterates through every cell (row by row), checks the cell value against the ranges defined in the text boxes, and then applies a color using a gradient scale:

  • Values near Minimum → Green
  • Values near Midpoint → Yellow
  • Values near Maximum → Red

This should work similarly to the color gradient shown in the attached/sample Excel worksheet.

Is it possible to achieve this through scripting or by creating a custom widget/control?

Any guidance or sample implementation would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: Working with BDC in the SAP Business AI Platform: The Data Analyst Perspective
entry.content:
As a data analyst working with SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC), you already rely on governed data models, consistent KPIs, and trusted semantics to produce analytics and insights. The Autonomous Enterprise strategy with the SAP Business AI Platform (BAIP) doesn’t change that foundation, it extends it.

What does change is how insights are generated, explained, and acted on. Analytics is no longer the final step in the data value chain. It becomes an active input into AI‑driven reasoning and execution, grounded in the same business context that you define and validate today. At the core of this shift is how BDC participates in the Contextualize and Reason services of BAIP, alongside the SAP Knowledge Graph. BDC provides the harmonized data foundation and business semantics, while the SAP Knowledge Graph encodes relationships and business context. Together, they form the trusted context layer that AI agents rely on to reason and act.

What stays the same

Your core analytical responsibilities remain intact. You continue to work with semantic models defined in SAP Datasphere, SAP BW, and SAP HANA Cloud, accessing SAP and non-SAP data, where business meaning is explicitly modeled. These models, together with master data governed through SAP Master Data Governance, form the structured input to the knowledge graph that AI relies on. SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) remains your primary environment for analysis, visualization, and planning.

The difference is that these analytical assets are no longer only consumed by humans, they are part of the contextual foundation used by AI agents.

What changes in practice

The most important shift is that AI now consumes the same semantic and contextual layer you work with. In the Autonomous Enterprise, AI agents do not generate insights from isolated datasets. They reason over BDC data products enriched with relationships captured in the SAP Knowledge Graph. When an AI agent produces a narrative, explains a deviation, or recommends an action, it operates on top of business objects, hierarchies, and dependencies that you already know.

This changes the validation process. Instead of questioning the origin of an AI insight, you can trace it back to governed data products and their relationships within the broader business context. Analytics shifts from being a downstream consumer of data to being a shared semantic contract between humans and AI.

How AI‑assisted analytics works with BDC

AI‑assisted insight generation doesn’t bypass your analytical models. It builds on them. Data products defined in BDC, whether sourced from SAP applications, BW objects, or Datasphere models, carry business meaning, lineage, and access rules. BAIP uses these data products as the context layer for AI reasoning. When AI generates narratives, summaries, or explanations, it does so against this governed semantic layer.

For you as a data analyst, this means faster exploration and explanation without sacrificing consistency. You spend less time reconciling definitions and more time interpreting results.

Impact on trust and validation

One of the biggest challenges with AI‑generated insights is trust. With BAIP, trust is achieved by design. Because AI agents rely on the same BDC‑managed semantics you use, AI outputs inherit the same KPI logic, hierarchies, and filters. Access controls and data policies are enforced consistently, whether the consumer is a dashboard, a planner, or an AI agent.

This dramatically reduces the need for parallel validation loops and manual reconciliation.

How this maps to BDC components

For data analysts, the component mapping is straightforward.

  • SAP Datasphere continues to be the primary place where analytical semantics are modeled and exposed as data products.
  • SAP BW continues to provide enterprise‑grade, historically rich business logic that remains authoritative for many KPIs.
  • SAP HANA Cloud supports advanced analytical queries and high‑performance access to data products.
  • SAP Analytics Cloud remains the main consumption layer for analysis, planning, and increasingly AI‑assisted insight generation.
  • SAP Master Data Governance ensures that master data consistency underpins all analytical and AI‑driven outcomes.

Together, these components form the trusted analytical foundation that AI agents consume through BAIP.

What to focus on now

As a data analyst, the most valuable preparation is not learning new AI tools. It is strengthening the analytical foundation AI will rely on. Focus on well‑defined KPIs, stable semantics, clear lineage, and reusable data products. The better your analytical models are today, the more reliable AI‑assisted insights will be tomorrow.

The takeaway

BAIP doesn’t replace analytics with AI. It connects analytics and AI through a shared semantic foundation. For data analysts, this means higher leverage: the models you build and validate do not just inform people, they inform AI‑driven decisions as well.

Join the Data Professionals community discussions to share analytical patterns, discuss AI‑assisted insights, and explore how others are evolving their BDC analytics foundations in the Autonomous Enterprise era.

This post is a follow up to the previous post From ‘AI in Applications’ to ‘AI on Applications’. It is the first in a series that explores how the SAP Business AI Platform (BAIP) impacts day‑to‑day work across key BDC personas, starting with the data analyst perspective.

entry.title: 5 Things I Wish I Knew Before Building My First SAC Story
entry.content:
When I first started working with SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC), I did what most people do - I jumped straight in. I had data, I had a business problem, and I figured the tool would guide me through the rest.

I was wrong.

I recently built a multi-page monitoring dashboard in SAC to help a team track data quality and integrity across multiple source systems. By the time it was live, I had made (and thankfully fixed) every single mistake on this list. Some of them cost me hours. One cost me a full rebuild.

Here are the 5 things I wish someone had told me before I started - shared with the hope that your journey is a little smoother than mine was.

1. Design for Your Audience First - Charts Second

The first version of my story opened with a table. It had every metric, every row, every column. It was complete. It was accurate. And absolutely nobody read it.

The turning point came when I stopped thinking like a data person and started thinking like my audience. I asked myself: what does this person need to understand in the first 10 seconds?

The answer was KPI tiles at the top - four bold numbers that immediately answered the most important questions. Everything else - the breakdowns, the trends, the category analysis - came below, for those who wanted to dig deeper.

Lesson: Lead with the "so what". Give your audience a landing pad before asking them to explore.

In SAC, numeric point charts with dynamic threshold coloring are your best tool for KPI tiles. Set conditional coloring so tiles automatically turn green, amber, or red - your audience should never have to read a number to know whether something needs attention.

2. Donut Charts Are Powerful - But Only With the Right Data

I used donut charts throughout my dashboard, and when used correctly they are brilliant. But in my early builds I used them for everything, and that is when things got confusing.

Donut charts do exactly one job well: showing a part-to-whole split with 2 to 3 segments. In my case, each donut answered a simple binary question - complete or missing? Two segments. Clean and readable.

Lesson: Choose your chart type based on the question you are answering, not the data you happen to have.

3. Multi-Page Stories Need a Clear Navigation Logic

My dashboard covers several different areas across multiple pages. When I first built it, I kept adding pages whenever I thought of something new to show. By page four, even I had to think twice about where things were.

The fix was simple in principle but painful in practice: I rebuilt the page structure so that each page answered exactly one question.

Lesson: Name your pages after the question they answer, not the data they contain.

In SAC, take the time to rename each page in the navigation panel. If you cannot decide which page a chart belongs on, that is usually a sign the page structure itself needs rethinking.

4. Understand Your Data Lineage Before You Build

This one cost me the most time - and the most frustration.

My dashboard pulls from multiple source systems into a central HANA database, and SAC connects to that. Straightforward enough, or so I thought. Midway through the build, I realised I had structured the model in a way that made certain calculated measures impossible. I had to go back, redesign the model, and rebuild a significant portion of the story from scratch.

Lesson: Before you open SAC, sketch your full data flow on paper and answer the five key questions below.

5. Define Your Color and Threshold Logic Early

The KPI tiles in my dashboard use color coding to instantly signal status to the viewer. I set this up late in the build. That was a mistake I would not repeat.

After I adjusted some threshold values post-launch, tiles that stakeholders had already learned to read suddenly showed different colors. The underlying data had not changed, but the visual had. People were confused, and I had to spend time rebuilding trust in the dashboard.

Lesson: Agree what red, amber, and green mean with your stakeholders - before you build a single widget.

Final Thoughts

Looking back, the technical skills such as  connecting data sources, building models, writing calculated measures were the parts I picked up fastest. The harder lessons were the thinking ones:

  • Who is actually reading this dashboard?
  • What single question does each page answer?
  • What does my data flow look like before I touch SAC?
  • What does each color mean to my audience?

If you can answer those four questions clearly before you open SAC, I genuinely believe you will build something your stakeholders use every day, not just on the day you present it.

I would love to hear what caught you out when you built your first SAC story. Drop a comment below - every lesson shared here saves someone else a rebuild!

 

Thank you for reading, and happy building!

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud Seamless Planning入門 - Part1 : Seamless Planningとは
entry.content:
このブログシリーズは、SAP Business Data CloudのSeamless Planningの全体像を一通り把握していただくことを目的としています。

 
このシリーズは、次の以下で構成されます。
Part1 : Seamless Planningとは（本ブログ）
Part2 :  Seamless Planningで計画モデルを作成してみよう
Part3 : Seamless PlanningでSAP Datasphereの実績ビューをライブバージョンで参照しよう
to be continued ...
 
本ブログ「Part1 : Seamless Planningとは」では、Seamless Planningで「何ができるのか」「どんなアーキテクチャなのか」「どんな場面で使われるのか」を解説します。
 

目次

  1. Seamless Planningとは
  2. Seamless Planningのメリット
  3. 主なユースケース
  4. アーキテクチャの全体像
  5. 利用開始の前提条件
     

1. Seamless Planningとは

Seamless Planningは、計画・分析・データ管理を一つの環境で実現する、SAPが提供するプランニングの仕組みです。計画の入力・計算・分析はSAP Analytics Cloudで行い、そのデータはデータ基盤のSAP Datasphereに直接格納されます。データの転送や同期なしに分析・予測・AI活用につなげることができます。

2. Seamless Planningのメリット

  • 計画ツールとデータウェアハウスが常に一体
    SAP Analytics Cloudで作成した計画データはSAP Datasphereに直接格納されるため、計画値の更新がそのままデータウェアハウスに反映されます。データのエクスポートや手動連携は不要です。計画担当者が数値を入力・公開すると、SAP Datasphere側のビューやレポートにも即時に反映されます。

  • 実績データとのリアルタイム比較が可能
    SAP S/4HANAなどの基幹システムからSAP Datasphereに取り込まれた実績データを、計画モデル内でリアルタイムに参照できます。「計画 対 実績」の差異分析を、データの複製なしに常に最新の状態で行えます。

  • AI/MLとの統合が容易
    SAP Business Data CloudではDatatbricksやSnowflakeなどの外部のオープンデータエコシステムとゼロコピーでデータの共有を行うことができます。計画データも同じ基盤上にあることで、時系列予測・機械学習モデルの結果を計画プロセスに組み込むことが追加のデータ複製なしに可能です。

3. 主なユースケース

Seamless Planningはさまざまな計画業務に対応しています。代表的なユースケースを紹介します。
  • 損益(P&L)計画
    売上・コスト・粗利などの財務計画をSAP Analytics Cloudで立案。SAP Datasphereに格納されたSAP S/4HANAの実績データとリアルタイムで比較しながら、予算や予測を策定します。

  • ドライバーベース計画とシナリオ分析
    売上数量・単価・為替レートなどのビジネスドライバーをもとに計算ロジックを組み、楽観・悲観・標準などの複数シナリオを並行して管理します。外部データ（物価指数・市場データ）もSAP Datasphereから直接参照して予測精度を高められます。

  • ローリング予測
    月次・四半期ごとに予測を更新するローリングフォーキャストも、SAP Datasphereの実績を直接参照することで最新の実績をすぐに取り込みながら実行できます。

  • xP&A（全社統合計画）
    財務計画だけでなく、人員計画・サプライチェーン計画・マーケティング予算など、複数部門の計画データをSAP Datasphere上で統合したり、他の計画モデルに対してライブで参照したりすることによって横断的な分析・調整を行うことができます。

 

4. アーキテクチャの全体像

ポイント：
1️⃣SAP Analytics Cloudで 定義した計画モデルのデータをSAP Datasphereに保管
2️⃣ SAP Datasphereにある実績のビューに対して計画モデルからライブバージョンとして接続
3️⃣ SAP BDC Connectで連携してきた外部データやAI予測データも同様に参照
4️⃣ 他の計画モデルの計画データに対してもライブバージョンとして接続して参照
 

5. 利用前の前提条件

Seamless Planningを利用するには、以下の条件を満たしている必要があります。
  • 同一データセンター：SAP Analytics CloudとSAP Datasphereが同じリージョンで稼働していること
  • SAP HANA Cloud基盤：SAP Analytics CloudテナントがSAP HANA Cloud上で動作していること（2024年中頃以降の新規テナントは標準対応）
  • 1対1テナント関係：SAP Analytics CloudテナントとSAP Datasphereテナントが1対1で接続されていること  
     

以上、Seamless Planningとは？を簡単に解説しました。次回のブログでは、実際にSeamless Planningモデルを作成し、Datasphere側でデータを確認する手順を解説します。


参考資料・ブログ

- シームレスな計画について | SAP Help Portal

- Seamless Planning with Datasphere & SAC: Seamless Planning in the Cloud
- Real-time steering with LIVE planning

entry.title: Using Input Controls in SAC Stories to Drive Dynamic Filtering
entry.content:
There is a moment when a dashboard stops being a static report and becomes a tool people actually want to  use. In SAP Analytics Cloud, Input Controls are often what makes that happen.

Input Controls let your audience filter what they see without you having to build a separate story for every team, every time period, or every system. One story. Many views. Zero duplication.

In this post I will walk through the three types I use most - dropdowns, date selectors, & linked page-wide filters and share how to decide which one to reach for and when.

                 

1. Dropdown Filters - The Workhorse

Dropdown filters are the most common Input Control in SAC, and for good reason. They give users a clean, familiar way to slice the data by a known list of values — a system name, a category, a status, a team.

The key decision is whether to use single-select or multi-select:

  • Single-select — use this when only one value makes sense at a time. For example, selecting the current reporting period or a specific data source to investigate.
  • Multi-select — use this when your audience needs to compare across values. For example, viewing two systems side by side to spot discrepancies.

                   

2. Date Range Selectors - Give Users Time Control

If your story tracks anything over time record counts, transfer volumes, quality scores then a date range selector is essential. Without one, your audience is stuck with whatever time window you hardcoded when you built the story.

The trap I fell into early on was giving users too much freedom. A date picker that allows any range sounds flexible, but in practice it leads to confusion when someone selects a five-year window and wonders why the chart looks flat.

Think about what decisions your audience is actually making:

  • Investigating a recent issue? Offer a narrow range - last 7 or 30 days.
  • Reviewing long-term trends? Offer a wider range - quarterly or annual.

3. Linked Filters - One Control, Whole Page

This is the most powerful pattern in SAC Input Controls, and the one that impresses stakeholders the most. A linked filter updates every widget on the page simultaneously from a single selection.

In my dashboard, selecting a data source from one dropdown instantly updates the KPI tiles, trend charts, completeness donuts, and detail tables - all in one click. No need to apply the same filter six times across six different charts.

The important thing to understand is scope: in SAC you can assign an Input Control to specific widgets, or to the entire page. Page-level assignment is almost always what you want for a monitoring or operational dashboard.

4. How to Decide What to Filter

The most common mistake I see and made myself is adding too many filters. More filters feels helpful but quickly becomes overwhelming. Users spend more time configuring the dashboard than reading it.

Two rules I now follow:

  • Filter dimensions, not measures. A dimension is a category (system name, data source, status). A measure is a number (record count, completeness %). Users almost never need to filter by a number directly — they need to filter by what that number belongs to.
  • Maximum three filters per page. More than three and the cognitive load outweighs the flexibility. If you feel you need more, it is usually a sign the page is trying to do too much.

Key takeaway: Ask yourself: what decision will my audience make using this filter? If you cannot answer clearly, the filter probably does not need to exist.

Final Thoughts

Input Controls transform SAC stories from dashboards people visit once into tools people rely on daily. The goal is not to give users maximum control, it is to give them exactly the right amount of control to answer their questions without friction.

Start simple: one dropdown, one date selector. See how your audience uses them. Add linked filters once you understand the questions your story needs to answer. And always ask - what decision is this filter supporting?

What Input Control patterns have worked well in your SAC stories? I would love to hear your approach in the comments below.

Thanks for reading -- happy filtering!

entry.title: Live Data Connectivity in SAP Analytics Cloud - Overview and Direction
entry.content:
Over the past year and a half we have been quietly introducing and expanding a new way to connect to data in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) and in this blog post I'm going to explain a little bit about why we're doing this, how it works and what the future of it looks like.

For as long as SAC has existed we have had the "Live Data Model" button in the modeler. This has been our traditional way of connecting to live data and it works via connecting to existing SAP Business Warehouse (BW) queries, S/4 HANA views, and SAP HANA calc views. This allows customers who have existing data and semantic models in their on their warehouse systems and HANA databases to reuse those existing semantic models in the context of analytics cloud. Some customers have BW queries that have been around for almost 2 decades and have used them with various client tools over the years, be they BEx Analyzer, Analysis Office, Webi, Lumira, and now SAC. That semantic content is safe and can continue to be used with each new generations of tools. This is also how the SAP Datasphere (DSP) SQL views and Analytic Models work.

Lately, we've been introducing a new way of connecting to data that's not necessarily so obvious, but has enormous impact; the live data connection. The way this works is that you start creating a normal SAC model and opt to create a new model from data; the "I need to create a model from data, so that I can craft my semantic model around my data" option. If the data source that you choose supports live data, you're given the option to import the data or to consume it live. In a nutshell we are taking advantage of the Smart Data Access (SDA) in SAP HANA, to use virtual tables as the basis of the fact table (aka data foundation) and take care of setting up the fact table type for you, behind the scenes.  This approach forms the basis for both the live data connection and  Seamless Planning. 

That’s it! There’s no magic, and the best part is you don’t have to decide at the very beginning of the workflow whether you want to create a model with imported data or with live data. Federated data is not just for data engineers anymore!

In the context of planning, we use the term "live versions." This is because planning models can have multiple versions of fact data.  In fact, you can add a live version to an existing planning model. Sometimes also colloquially we refer to the live data connection as "SQL live" even though that is a misnomer, because not all supported data sources use SQL interfaces.

Who classic Live Data Models are for and when

• Users who have existing semantic models in S/4, BW, or HANA (e.g., BW queries or HANA calc views) that they want to re-use.

SAC acts as a runtime client in this scenario and the semantic model is a black box from SAC’s perspective, so if you want to control the semantic model from the SAC side, this is not the best choice.

Who Live Data Connectivity is for and when

• Users building analytic models, when they want to ensure that the remote data source is the single source of truth.
• Planners, when they don’t want to replicate data into the actuals version, especially when that source is a large data lake.  This is also how Seamless Planning works.
• Others who don’t want the complexity of scheduling load jobs. For example, suppose you have a shared Excel file where several users are collectively editing quarterly risk analysis. If you wanted a risk analysis dashboard based on that data, you might find it very convenient if that dashboard is always based on the current values in the Excel table.

Why we're building out the live data connection

The traditional Analytics Cloud data model required you to replicate or import your data into SAC. In some respects, this is very simple. You simply connect to the data, copy it into Analytics Cloud, and then you build your semantic model on top of that for use in Analytics Cloud. If you want to update this data later, then you can always set up and schedule a load job to periodically update the data. You can bring data in from multiple sources into the same semantic model. Etcetera, etcetera.

But… We run into certain limitations in certain scenarios. For example:

• A customer may have a very large data lake. They want to do planning based on the data in this data lake, but don’t want to replicate gigabytes of data into SAC.
• Some sources are updated on a continuous basis and the replicated data might not reflect the real-time state.
• Some users may not want to be bothered with the additional effort of scheduling updates. Sure, scheduling load jobs in SAC is a far cry from data engineering, but it is still cognitive load and effort that brings minimal value in some situations.

For such situations, live data connections are a clear quality-of-life improvement.


Wait! What about DSP’s Remote Tables and BDC Zero Copy?

DSP’s Remote Tables and BDC Zero Copy are complementary to live data connections. Think of live data connections as "Federation Light." It does not require a data engineer skill set, nor does it require your SAC tenant to be in a BDC seamless planning formation. It augments DSP’s Remote Tables and BDC Zero Copy and does not replace the data engineer toolchain.

 

Which Connections are supported for live

As of mid-2026, live data connectivity is supported for:
• Google BigQuery - was the first data source enabled with this capability.
• Microsoft Azure SQL Database - added in quarterly release (QRC) 2026.Q2.
• Snowflake - added in QRC 2026.Q1.

Support for SharePoint Lists and Excel files in SharePoint are planned for QRC 2026.Q4. Other sources—especially the popular cloud data lakes—are planned to follow.

Authentication

Authentication to the remote data source is currently only supported for a service or technical user, as is the case with DSP remote tables. This means that if you need to use record level data access rules from the original source, you will have to replicate the access tables in the source into SAC data access controls (DAC). This is the same behavior that we have in DSP today. We are currently working on enabling user propagation to the remote source, which would allow you to use the remote data access tables with live connectivity in SAC without replicating your DAC. This will be a centralized BDC service for both SAC and DSP, though it will be available first in SAC. It is not on the Roadmap Explorer yet, but when we have a clearer idea of the release quarter, we will publish it.


Getting Started with Live Data Connections

To get started with live data connections, you want to ensure that your SAC tenant is on HANA Cloud and that live data connections are enabled on your tenant.

You can find the HANA version by checking the About page. You can find the About page by locating your user profile button, clicking it, and then selecting About at the bottom of the popup. The HANA version will be displayed on the About page. If your HANA version is not yet HANA Cloud, be advised that we are in the midst of migrating customer tenants over and it should be available in the course of the next few months, but you can also check with your account team to see when your tenant will be migrated.

Next, make sure that live data connections are enabled on your tenant. Doing this will enable SAC models to use live data when the data source supports it. To do this, go to the Administration module, select the Data Source Configuration tab, and ensure that Enable Data Access Agent is toggled on. This will enable the microservice that enables live data connectivity for most sources.

Now you are ready to set up the data sources in the Connections module.

Important note about connections that use the Cloud Agent or Open Connectors for import connectivity

Any source using a JDBC connection via the Cloud Agent or Open Connectors for import connectivity will behave a little bit differently between live and imported. This is because the import connection runs over the Cloud Agent via JDBC, or via BTP Open Connectors, while the live connection runs directly from SAC to the data source, cloud-to-cloud.

For example, if you have configured your Cloud Agent for import but not enabled the Enable Data Access Agent option, then you will only see the option to import data. Conversely, if you have not configured JDBC import via the Cloud Agent but have toggled Enable Data Access Agent on, then you will only have live connectivity. Also, the non-JDBC (live) connection needs its own entry in the Connections module. The tenant that I took the screenshots from does not have a Cloud Agent connected to it, but it does have live connectivity enabled and a Snowflake driver and an Azure SQL driver enabled. Therefore, on my tenant I only see the live option.

This behavior currently affects Snowflake and Microsoft Azure SQL Database connectivity. Google BigQuery is cloud-to-cloud for both import and live connectivity and is not affected. The legacy Microsoft SharePoint Excel connection runs over Open Connectors, so the upcoming Microsoft SharePoint Excel connection will have this behavior, but only temporarily. It is not in the Roadmap Explorer yet, but by mid-2027 we plan to release a new, better Microsoft SharePoint Excel connection that does not use Open Connectors. There is no legacy Microsoft SharePoint Lists connection for import, so until we can add the import option, it will be live only.

Useful Links

KBA 2928379

Configuring a Google BigQuery live connection

Configuring a Microsoft Azure SQL Database live connection

Configuring a Snowflake live connection

An excellent (external) blog post by Rafael Sachs about using Snowflake as a live source in SAC

entry.title: Smarter, More Flexible, More Powerful: SAP Analytics Cloud Composites Innovation Roundup
entry.content:
Composites are keeping up with the pace of innovation in SAP Analytics Cloud — and honestly, I think the last 18 months are proof of that. We've shipped meaningful capabilities across every single quarter: governance features that make composite libraries manageable at scale, scripting improvements that give developers real flexibility, and integrations that extend composites well beyond the boundaries of SAC itself. The momentum is real, and it's not slowing down.

I would like to walk you through the highlights from 2025 Q1 through 2026 Q2, so you can quickly see what's new and how it may change the way your team builds and consumes analytics content.

 2026 Q2  · Composite Versioning and Others

Version Control for Composite Artefacts

Perhaps the most anticipated governance feature for composite designers: version history. Designers can now manage up to 10 versions of a composite. The current version remains active in any consuming story, while previous versions can be opened in read-only mode for review — without touching anything live. If a past version needs to be restored, a backup of the current version is automatically created before the rollback takes effect.

All versions — current and historical — always reference the latest state of models and shared resources, and transports between tenants carry only the active version. This feature fundamentally changes how teams can iterate on composites in production environments.

Interface Properties in Composite Calculations

Dynamic analytics scenarios — think threshold-based visualizations or configurable reference lines — can now be built using calculations that reference composite interface properties, with no scripting required. Story designers set those values per composite properties so a single composite can support multiple scenarios through configuration alone. Development effort drops, reuse goes up.

Composites in Lite Viewer

Composites are now fully supported in Lite Viewer, enabling faster story loading for users  — making composite-based content more accessible across the organization.

Importing SAP-Delivered Composites from Intelligent Applications

Users can now import SAP-delivered composites from intelligent applications in SAP Business Data Cloud directly into their own stories. This means your team can build custom stories on top of SAP's vetted composite components, benefiting from ongoing SAP updates while retaining the freedom to extend and customize.

Deep Copy Support for Composites with Data Models

During installation of SAP-managed intelligent applications, composite widgets and data models are replicated across the package, enabling reuse across multiple stories from a single installation. 

 See it in Action:

 

2026 Q1  ·  Scripting Improvements and Designer Experience

Optional Arguments in Composite Interface Functions

Designers can now define optional arguments in composite interface functions, appended after mandatory ones. When a script function is auto-exposed, changes to its optional arguments are automatically synced with the interface function. When new optional arguments are added, existing consuming stories require no adjustments — backward compatibility is preserved automatically.

Overwriting Composites with the Same Technical ID

Composite designers can now save a modified composite back over the original technical ID, preserving identity in the system while evolving the content. Consuming stories must be re-opened and re-saved to pick up the change — a small but meaningful quality-of-life improvement.

Auto-Incorporation of Composite Data Sources

When a composite is inserted onto the story canvas, its associated data sources are now added automatically — limited to sources the user has access to and that haven't already been added. This eliminates the prior manual step and reduces the risk of designer error during story assembly.

Chart Colors and Patterns Carry Over from Composites

Chart colors and patterns defined within a composite are now preserved in consuming stories, removing a manual reconciliation step and ensuring that composite-level design intent is carried through end-to-end.

For details you can refer to:

What's New in SAP Analytics Cloud | Deep Dive with Product Experts | Q1 2026

2025 Q4  ·  Discoverability and Script Reuse

Where-Used List for Composites

A where-used list is now available in the file repository and at composite design time, showing which stories consume a given composite along with name, description, owner, creation date, and modification metadata. Columns are sortable and the list is searchable — making composite lifecycle management tractable at enterprise scale.

Automatic Exposure of Script Object Functions

Custom script object functions can now be automatically exposed as reusable interface functions, eliminating the need to manually copy scripts across stories when sharing logic between composites and consuming stories.

 For details you can refer to:

What's New in SAP Analytics Cloud | Deep Dive with Product Experts | Q4 2025

2025 Q3  ·  Scripting Depth and Design-Time Flexibility

Script Access for Composite Properties

Composite properties can now be read and assigned in the script editor and calculation editor, with errors and reference lists supported — bringing composite property scripting to parity with how other object properties are handled in the SAC scripting environment.

Page-Size Setting Improvements

Composite design-time page dimensions are now configurable across a wide range: width from 128px to 7680px, and height from 32px to 7680px. For composites that need to fit precisely into specific layout contexts, this granularity matters.

 For details you can refer to:

What's New in SAP Analytics Cloud | Deep Dive with Product Experts | Q3 2025

2025 Q2  ·  Visual Fidelity and UX Alignment

Threshold Support Within Composite Definitions

Visual thresholds can now be defined and embedded within the composite itself, operating independently from story-level thresholds — no risk of cross-composite interference. The initial scope covers chart widgets with measure-based thresholds; table and dimension-based assigned color support is planned for subsequent releases.

Fixed vs. Dynamic Composite Size

A new fixed sizing mode joins the existing dynamic default, giving designers pixel-precise control over composite dimensions for layout-critical scenarios where consistency across different story canvases is required.

Unified Styling and Builder Panel Experience

The composite properties panel has been integrated into the Styling panel, and composite model configuration has been moved into the Builder panel — aligning composites with the interaction pattern used for charts and tables, and reducing cognitive overhead for story designers who work across widget types.

 For details you can refer to:

What's New in SAP Analytics Cloud | Deep Dive with Product Experts | Q2 2025

2025 Q1  ·  Foundational Capability Expansion

Global Calculations in Composite Widgets

Account, dimension, and cross-calculations can now be defined directly within composite widgets. Custom widgets with applicable calculation support are also covered. This closes a significant capability gap that had limited how analytically rich a self-contained composite could be.

Flow Layout Panel and Slider Widgets

The composite widget palette now includes Flow Layout Panel, Slider, and Range Slider. Flow Layout Panel enables composites that adapt gracefully to different screen sizes. Sliders enable interactive, dynamic composites — for instance, what-if simulation scenarios where users adjust inputs and see results update in real time.

Linked Analysis Support for Composite Target Widgets

Composites can now participate in linked analysis as target widgets. When a source widget drives a filter or selection, composite widgets — and the charts, tables, custom widgets, and text elements within them that share the same data source — will respond accordingly.

Option to Disable Model Configuration in Composites

A new Global Settings option lets composite owners lock down data model configuration, preventing story designers from substituting models when consistency must be enforced. It's unchecked by default, preserving existing flexibility.

 For details you can refer to:

What's New in SAP Analytics Cloud | Deep Dive with Product Experts | Q1 2025

Summary and Outlook

Stepping back across these 6 quarters of release notes, three clear themes emerge:

  • Governance and lifecycle management — Versioning, where-used lists, transport controls, and deep copy support are progressively turning composites from ad hoc reuse artifacts into properly governed, enterprise-grade content components.
  • Developer and designer experience — Optional arguments, auto-exposure of script functions, auto-incorporation of data sources, and unified panel UX are all reducing friction and aligning composites with the quality-of-life expectations of modern analytics developers.
  • Integration with the broader SAP ecosystem — Lite Viewer support, SAP Business Data Cloud intelligent application imports, and Datasphere model integration are broadening composites capabilities across the entire SAP Business Data Cloud, with full support for Datasphere models and an expanding set of intelligent applications

For analytics teams building on SAP Analytics Cloud, these innovations collectively lower the cost of building reusable content, raise the ceiling of what composites can express, and provide the governance infrastructure needed to manage composite libraries at enterprise scale.

We are continuously advancing composite capabilities through ongoing innovation. Please stay tuned for further updates !
